proposals.title: Intra-Family Burden of Homozygous Loss-of-Function Variants: A Controlled Sequencing and Functional Validation Study
proposals.motivation: While population studies suggest an excess of homozygous LoF variants is deleterious, these signals are confounded by population stratification, genetic drift, and shared environment. A direct, controlled test within families is needed to isolate the causal impact of homozygosity from background genetic variation. This is critical for understanding the penetrance of recessive variants and for interpreting genetic risk in clinical and personal genomics contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: Within a family, siblings with a higher burden of rare, homozygous LoF variants, after controlling for the shared parental genetic background, will exhibit measurable phenotypic detriment across quantitative health traits. These putatively deleterious homozygous LoF variants will demonstrably disrupt gene function in cellular models.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-genome sequencing on 500 parent-offspring quads (2,000 individuals) from diverse biobanks. Using a modified burden test, we will calculate an individual's residual homozygous LoF burden relative to their family's expected distribution. We will then perform sibling-difference association analyses for a panel of deep phenotypic measures (e.g., metabolomics, clinical lab values, cognitive scores). Top-associated variants from discordant sibling pairs will be validated by generating iPSCs from these siblings, differentiating them into relevant cell types (e.g., neurons, hepatocytes), and assessing functional impact via RNA-seq and targeted proteomic assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, within-family evidence linking excess homozygous LoF burden to phenotypic detriment, rigorously controlling for confounders. It will generate a validated set of high-impact recessive variants and establish a framework for using family-based designs to assess the functional penetrance of putatively deleterious genotypes.
proposals.novelty: The study moves beyond population-level correlations by using the family as a natural controlled experiment, directly testing selection against homozygosity. It uniquely integrates sibling-difference genetic epidemiology with downstream cellular validation of specific variant effects in an isogenic background.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Integration of Rare Coding Variants and Time-to-Event Health Outcomes in a Large Prospective Cohort
proposals.motivation: Large-scale exome and genome sequencing has identified millions of rare coding variants, but their association with disease risk is often assessed using prevalent case-control designs, which can be confounded by survival and disease duration. Prospective cohorts with longitudinal follow-up provide a powerful, unbiased framework to study the impact of genetic variation on disease onset and progression over time. Leveraging such a resource is critical to move beyond static risk estimates and understand the dynamic genetic architecture of complex diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating rare coding variants, including those of uncertain significance, with longitudinal health data will identify novel genetic determinants of disease incidence and progression, and that these time-to-event associations will be more powerful and biologically informative than standard cross-sectional analyses.
proposals.method: We will perform a phenome-wide, time-to-event analysis within a large prospective cohort (e.g., UK Biobank, Michigan Genomics Initiative) with linked exome/genome data. For each individual, we will aggregate rare (MAF<0.1%) predicted loss-of-function and missense variants within genes and constrained genomic regions. We will apply Cox proportional hazards models, adjusted for principal components and relevant covariates, to test for associations between these variant sets and the time-to-first diagnosis of hundreds of quantitative traits and disease endpoints. Secondary analyses will explore non-proportional hazards and gene-environment interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a comprehensive map of rare variant associations with disease incidence, providing novel insights into the etiology of diseases where rare variants play a significant role. It will demonstrate the utility of longitudinal modeling for genetic association studies, potentially increasing power for variant discovery and offering more clinically relevant risk trajectories. The findings will prioritize genes and biological pathways for functional follow-up and therapeutic targeting.
proposals.novelty: While Abecasis and others have pioneered large-scale rare variant association studies (RVAS), this proposal uniquely applies rigorous time-to-event survival analysis to rare variant sets across the phenome, moving beyond binary case-control status to model the dynamic relationship between genetics and health outcomes.

proposals.title: Reference-free co-estimation of haplotype phase and ancestral segments in admixed genomes from long-read sequencing
proposals.motivation: Accurate haplotype phasing and local ancestry inference are critical for admixture mapping and rare variant association studies in diverse populations. However, current methods rely heavily on reference panels of phased haplotypes, which are often incomplete or non-existent for underrepresented ancestries, leading to biased or failed analyses. The increasing availability of long-read sequencing data provides an opportunity to overcome this dependency by directly observing long-range haplotypes within a study cohort.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint statistical model can leverage the long-range information in population-scale long-read sequencing data to co-estimate haplotype phase and ancestral haplotype structure de novo, without requiring an external reference panel, achieving accuracy comparable to panel-dependent methods in well-studied populations and superior performance in underrepresented groups.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational system based on a hierarchical clustering and hidden Markov model (HMM). First, we will identify long, identical-by-descent (IBD) segments shared across individuals within the cohort using a minhash-based clustering approach on phased SNP/indel calls from long reads. These clusters will form the building blocks of an internal, cohort-specific ancestral haplotype library. A factorial HMM will then perform joint inference, modeling each individual's diploid genome as a mosaic of these ancestral haplotype blocks, simultaneously resolving phase and local ancestry. The model will be optimized using an expectation-maximization algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, reference-free framework for generating high-quality phased haplotypes and ancestry-specific segments directly from long-read data of any admixed population. It will empower more powerful and equitable rare variant association studies and admixture mapping by removing the reference panel bottleneck, directly addressing disparities in genetic research highlighted by Dr. Abecasis's prior work on diagnostic rates and access.
proposals.novelty: While existing tools perform phasing or local ancestry inference separately, often with panel dependency, our method's core novelty is the fully integrated, reference-free co-estimation of both parameters, building the ancestral reference directly from the data via hierarchical clustering of long-read-derived haplotypes.

proposals.title: Robust Discovery of Putatively Protective Rare Coding Variants via Fine-Scale Ancestry-Conditioned Association Testing
proposals.motivation: Identifying rare protective variants is crucial for understanding disease biology and therapeutic target discovery, but standard case-control association studies are severely confounded by population stratification. This is especially problematic for rare variants, where subtle differences in ancestry between cases and controls can create spurious protective signals, undermining replicability and downstream validation. Current methods like principal component adjustment are often insufficient at fine scales, particularly in admixed cohorts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that performing association tests for protection within genetically homogeneous clusters, defined by high-resolution ancestry assignment, will effectively eliminate stratification artifacts and yield a robust set of replicable, putatively protective rare coding variants.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will use a reference-based ancestry assignment algorithm (e.g., RFMix) and fine-scale principal component analysis on genome-wide array or sequencing data to cluster individuals into homogeneous genetic background groups. Second, within each cluster, we will perform burden and SKAT-O tests on rare (MAF<0.01) coding variants, comparing frequencies between cases and controls while conditioning on local ancestry coordinates. Significance will be aggregated across clusters via meta-analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust methodological framework for protective variant discovery that directly addresses the primary confounder of population stratification. It will generate a high-confidence catalog of putatively protective rare variants for complex diseases, enhancing target discovery for functional follow-up and drug development.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard stratification correction, which treats ancestry as a covariate, our method explicitly creates matched genetic backgrounds for comparison, a direct, ancestry-conditioned approach not commonly applied to the specific problem of protective variant discovery in rare variant association studies.

proposals.title: Genome-wide Scan for Protective Homozygous Loss-of-Function Variants via Case-Control Depletion Analysis
proposals.motivation: Large-scale genomic studies have primarily focused on identifying disease-causing variants, but a systematic search for protective genetic factors remains underexplored. The discovery of homozygous loss-of-function (LoF) variants that confer resilience to common diseases could reveal novel biological pathways for therapeutic intervention. This is particularly timely given the expansion of large biobanks and disease-specific cohorts with exome/genome sequencing data, enabling well-powered case-control comparisons for rare homozygous genotypes.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific homozygous LoF variants are significantly depleted in individuals with specific common diseases compared to matched controls, providing statistical evidence for a protective effect against disease pathogenesis.
proposals.method: We will perform a genome-wide burden test for depletion, analyzing high-quality homozygous LoF variants (e.g., stop-gain, essential splice, frameshift) from exome/genome sequencing data in large case-control cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank, FinnGen). For each gene, we will apply a logistic regression model testing for underrepresentation of homozygous LoF carriers in cases versus ancestry-matched controls, adjusting for relevant covariates. Significance will be assessed using a Firth-corrected likelihood-ratio test to handle rare genotypes, with a gene-based burden p-value threshold informed by the number of tested genes and LoF variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first systematic catalog of homozygous LoF variants with statistically significant protective associations against major diseases, moving beyond tolerance to demonstrable benefit. Identified variants and genes will provide strong candidates for functional validation to elucidate mechanisms of genetic resilience. The findings could directly inform the development of novel therapeutic strategies that mimic the protective effect of these LoF alleles.
proposals.novelty: While previous work (e.g., gnomAD) cataloged LoF variants tolerated in the general population, this study explicitly tests for their *protective* effect against disease via formal case-control depletion analysis, a hypothesis-driven approach distinct from population frequency filtering. It leverages the specific analytical framework for detecting protective variants but applies it to the understudied context of homozygous, rather than heterozygous, LoF genotypes.

proposals.title: Systematic Discovery of Putative Protective Coding Variants via Case-Control Depletion Analysis in Large-Scale Biobanks
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully identified thousands of risk-increasing variants for complex diseases, but the systematic discovery of protective variants remains under-explored. Identifying genetic factors that confer resistance to disease is crucial for understanding disease biology and for developing novel therapeutic strategies, including mimicking protective loss-of-function. Current rare variant association studies (RVAS) are primarily powered to detect risk effects, leaving a significant gap in our understanding of the full genetic architecture of disease susceptibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coding variants with a statistically significant depletion in disease cases compared to population controls represent a rich source of putative protective alleles, and that aggregating such signals across genes and pathways will reveal novel biological mechanisms of disease resistance.
proposals.method: We will perform a genome-wide scan for protective variants by analyzing exome/genome sequencing data from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, All of Us). For each phenotype, we will test for an unusually low allele frequency in cases versus controls using a Fisher's exact test framework, focusing on rare (MAF < 1%) and low-frequency (1% < MAF < 5%) coding variants. We will then apply gene-based burden and SKAT-O tests specifically configured to detect protective effects by testing for a lower cumulative variant burden in cases. Significant hits will be functionally annotated using pre-2026 tools like VEP and integrated with protein structure and pathway databases.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic catalog of coding variants associated with protection against a wide range of common diseases. It will provide new biological insights into disease mechanisms by highlighting genes and pathways where disruption is beneficial. The findings will prioritize novel targets for drug development aimed at mimicking these protective genetic effects.
proposals.novelty: While existing RVAS methods can theoretically detect protective effects, they are not optimized for this purpose. This proposal explicitly reframes the analytical framework to prioritize the detection of variant depletion, a non-standard but powerful approach for protective allele discovery that leverages the same data but asks a complementary question to standard risk-focused analyses.

proposals.title: Enhancing Rare Variant Association Power and Portability Through a Global, Multi-Population LD Reference Panel
proposals.motivation: Current rare variant association studies (RVAS) and fine-mapping efforts rely heavily on linkage disequilibrium (LD) reference panels from limited populations, primarily of European ancestry (e.g., HapMap, early 1000 Genomes). This creates a critical bottleneck for genetic research in under-represented populations, reducing power for association detection and limiting the portability of polygenic risk scores. Addressing this disparity is essential for equitable genomic medicine and for uncovering population-specific genetic architecture of disease.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating high-coverage LD data from diverse global populations into existing RVAS and fine-mapping tools will significantly improve association power for rare and low-frequency coding variants in non-European groups and enhance the biological interpretation of discovered loci across ancestries.
proposals.method: We will extend the computational framework of RVAS tools (e.g., SAIGE, REGENIE) and fine-mapping methods to natively integrate LD from the expanded 1000 Genomes Project, the NHLBI TOPMed program, and the Genome Aggregation Database (gnomAD) v3.1. This involves developing a new LD matrix storage and query system using sparse matrix compression and population-specific tagging. We will implement a Bayesian fine-mapping algorithm, such as SuSiE, modified to use multiple, population-stratified LD references simultaneously, allowing for cross-population colocalization analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source software module that enables researchers to perform more powerful and accurate rare variant association tests and credible set refinement in diverse cohorts. It will directly address known health disparities in genetic research by providing the necessary reference data infrastructure for understudied populations, leading to more inclusive variant discovery and risk prediction.
proposals.novelty: While multiple LD references exist, their systematic integration into a unified, computationally efficient framework for rare variant analysis across diverse populations is lacking. This proposal moves beyond simply providing more data to creating a method that dynamically selects and weights population-appropriate LD structures to optimize association testing and interpretation.

proposals.title: Functional Prioritization and Validation of Germline Clonal Hematopoiesis Risk Loci Using Massively Parallel Reporter Assays and CRISPR Perturbation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified multiple loci associated with germline predisposition to clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP), a condition linked to hematologic cancer and cardiovascular disease risk. However, the causal variants and their molecular mechanisms at most of these loci remain unknown, hindering biological insight and therapeutic target discovery. This gap is critical to address, as CHIP represents a major, age-related risk factor with significant public health implications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating statistical fine-mapping of CHIP GWAS signals with high-throughput in silico variant effect prediction will identify specific candidate causal non-coding variants, which can be functionally validated using in vitro assays to reveal their regulatory impact on gene expression in hematopoietic progenitor cells.
proposals.method: We will first perform statistical fine-mapping of known CHIP GWAS loci using SuSiE and PAINTOR to define credible sets of candidate causal variants. These variants will be prioritized using a proteomics-informed large language model (e.g., AlphaMissense framework adapted for non-coding effects) to predict disruptive regulatory effects. Top-priority variants from non-coding credible sets will be cloned into a massively parallel reporter assay (MPRA) library and transfected into human hematopoietic cell lines (e.g., K562) to quantify allele-specific effects on transcriptional activity. For coding variants in genes like TET2 and DNMT3A, we will perform CRISPR-Cas9 base editing in primary CD34+ cells to assess functional consequences on differentiation and clonal expansion.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond statistical association to establish causal mechanisms for specific genetic variants underlying germline CHIP risk. It will deliver a prioritized list of functionally validated variants and their target genes, providing a foundational resource for understanding CHIP biology. The findings will enable more accurate polygenic risk scoring and highlight novel pathways for potential therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identified CHIP-associated loci, this proposal uniquely integrates state-of-the-art in silico variant effect prediction models, developed for coding regions, with targeted high-throughput functional assays specifically for non-coding germline CHIP variants, a largely unexplored area. This systematic, assay-guided approach to validation at population-identified loci is novel for the CHIP field.

proposals.title: Cross-Ancestry Genome-Wide Association Study of Clonal Hematopoiesis of Indeterminate Potential (CHIP) and Its Phenotypic Correlates
proposals.motivation: Clonal Hematopoiesis of Indeterminate Potential (CHIP) is a common age-related condition driven by somatic mutations in hematopoietic stem cells, strongly associated with hematologic cancer risk, cardiovascular disease, and all-cause mortality. However, as of 2025, large-scale genetic studies of CHIP have been predominantly conducted in individuals of European ancestry, limiting our understanding of its genetic architecture, prevalence, and phenotypic impact in diverse populations. This gap perpetuates health disparities and hinders the development of globally relevant risk prediction models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) the genetic determinants of CHIP risk and its allele frequency spectrum differ significantly across ancestries, and (2) the phenotypic associations of CHIP, particularly with cardiometabolic and inflammatory traits, are modified by genetic ancestry and population-specific environmental factors.
proposals.method: We will perform a cross-ancestry meta-analysis of genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for CHIP, leveraging whole-exome or whole-genome sequencing data from large, deeply phenotyped cohorts including TOPMed, UK Biobank, and All of Us. CHIP will be defined by the presence of somatic mutations in a predefined set of driver genes (e.g., DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1) at a variant allele fraction ≥2%. We will use established rare-variant association testing frameworks (e.g., SAIGE, REGENIE) to identify common and rare germline genetic variants associated with CHIP risk, stratified by and combined across ancestries. Phenotypic associations will be tested using multivariable regression models adjusted for relevant covariates, with a focus on traits underrepresented in prior European-centric studies.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first comprehensive catalog of germline genetic risk factors for CHIP across multiple ancestries, directly addressing a critical gap in genomic medicine. It will quantify the burden and clinical impact of CHIP in non-European populations, providing essential data for equitable clinical risk assessment. The findings will enable more powerful polygenic risk scores and inform the biological mechanisms linking somatic mosaicism to age-related diseases.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established CHIP associations in mostly European cohorts, this proposal systematically characterizes its genetic and phenotypic landscape in diverse ancestries, testing for ancestry-specific effects and interactions that have not been explored at scale. It applies state-of-the-art rare-variant association methods, a core strength of the Abecasis lab, to the novel domain of somatic-germline interaction.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Causal Pathways from Clonal Hematopoiesis Driver Mutations to Distinct Cardiovascular and Inflammatory Phenotypes
proposals.motivation: Clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) is a common age-related condition associated with increased risk of hematologic cancer, cardiovascular disease, and all-cause mortality. Pre-2026 genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and exome sequencing have identified specific CHIP driver genes (e.g., DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1, JAK2) and linked them broadly to inflammatory and lipid traits, but the precise, gene-specific mechanistic pathways remain poorly understood, limiting targeted intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct CHIP driver mutations confer specific downstream molecular signatures in blood cells, which mediate their unique associations with clinical traits such as altered lipid profiles, elevated inflammatory markers, and blood cell indices.
proposals.method: We will perform a large-scale, integrated rare variant association study in cohorts like UK Biobank, leveraging exome/genome sequencing and extensive phenotyping. We will associate specific CHIP mutations (VAF > 2%) with deep molecular phenotypes (plasma proteomics, single-cell RNA-seq from blood, detailed blood cytometry) using regression models adjusting for age and other confounders. Causal mediation analysis will then test whether these molecular intermediates statistically explain the gene-specific associations with clinical lab traits (e.g., LDL-C, CRP, monocyte count).
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond gene-trait associations to map the functional pathways connecting specific somatic mutations to disease risk. It will identify candidate mediating proteins and cell states for experimental validation, providing a mechanistic framework to explain the heterogeneity of CHIP clinical outcomes and nominating potential targets for gene-specific therapeutic strategies.
proposals.novelty: While CHIP-trait associations are known, this study uniquely integrates detailed molecular phenotyping at scale with causal inference methods to systematically deconstruct the 'black box' between driver mutation and clinical outcome, assigning specific biological mechanisms to specific genes.

proposals.title: Integrated Analysis of Clonal Hematopoiesis and Solid Tumor Somatic Mutations Reveals Shared Etiology and Modifies Cancer Risk
proposals.motivation: Clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) is a common age-related phenomenon associated with increased risk of hematologic cancers and cardiovascular disease. Pre-2026, large-scale studies of somatic mutations in solid tumors and CHIP have largely been conducted in isolation, despite emerging evidence that they may share environmental or genetic predispositions. A unified analysis is critical to understand if CHIP acts as a sentinel for broader somatic mutational processes, influencing solid tumor development and patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals with CHIP harbor a distinct spectrum of somatic mutations in their solid tumors and that germline genetic variants influence the co-occurrence of CHIP and specific solid tumor mutations, revealing shared biological mechanisms of somatic evolution.
proposals.method: We will integrate whole-exome or genome sequencing data from blood (for CHIP calling) and matched solid tumors from cohorts like The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) and UK Biobank. Using rare variant association study frameworks and mixed-effects models, we will test for associations between CHIP status (and specific driver genes like DNMT3A, TET2) and the burden and signature of somatic mutations in tumors. We will perform genome-wide association studies (GWAS) to identify germline variants predisposing to both CHIP and specific tumor mutational patterns, leveraging Abecasis group's expertise in large-scale genetic analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first population-scale map of relationships between CHIP and solid tumor somatic genomics. It will identify germline modifiers of somatic evolution across tissues and determine if CHIP status provides prognostic information beyond standard clinical markers for cancer patients. The findings could redefine risk stratification and suggest novel prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While CHIP and cancer genomics are mature fields, their deliberate integration in the same individuals to discover trans-tissue rules of somatic mutation is novel. The approach moves beyond cataloging independent events to modeling the human body as a mosaic of interacting somatic genomes.

proposals.title: Ancestry-Adaptive STAAR: Extending Robust Rare Variant Association Testing to Diverse and Admixed Populations
proposals.motivation: Rare variant association studies (RVAS) have predominantly been conducted in populations of European ancestry, limiting the generalizability of findings and exacerbating health disparities. The STAAR (variant-Set Test for Association using Annotation infoRmation) framework is a powerful method for RVAS that integrates variant functional annotations, but its performance and optimal configuration in diverse ancestries and admixed populations remain largely uncharacterized. This gap hinders the equitable application of genetic discovery and the identification of ancestry-specific disease risk factors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the STAAR framework to explicitly account for population-specific allele frequency spectra, linkage disequilibrium (LD) patterns, and ancestry-correlated annotation distributions will yield more robust and powerful rare variant association tests across diverse global populations.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale, deeply sequenced cohorts with diverse ancestries (e.g., TOPMed, All of Us) to empirically characterize ancestry-specific properties of rare variants. We will develop and test ancestry-adaptive extensions to STAAR, including: 1) population-specific weighting schemes based on local ancestry and allele frequency, 2) annotation principal component analysis to correct for ancestry-correlated annotation biases, and 3) a mixed-model kernel to account for complex relatedness and admixture within and between groups. Performance will be benchmarked via simulations and real-trait analyses against standard STAAR and other RVAS methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, open-source software package for ancestry-robust rare variant association testing, directly enabling more inclusive genetic studies. It will produce a set of best-practice guidelines for applying STAAR in diverse cohorts and deliver novel insights into the genetic architecture of complex traits across ancestries, moving the field toward more equitable genomic medicine.
proposals.novelty: While STAAR is a leading RVAS method, its systematic adaptation for non-European and admixed populations is a critical, unmet need. Our approach moves beyond simple stratification to integrate ancestry-aware modeling directly into the statistical framework, which is non-trivial given the complex interplay between population structure, rare variant spectra, and functional annotations.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Framework for Rare Variant Association Studies Incorporating Variant Age as an Informative Prior
proposals.motivation: Rare variant association studies (RVAS) are underpowered, especially for non-coding variants, due to extreme allele frequency thresholds and the challenge of functional annotation. Pre-2026, variant age estimates derived from haplotype structure and population genetics models have been used to infer selection pressure and pathogenicity, but not systematically integrated into association testing frameworks. Leveraging this temporal dimension could prioritize evolutionarily recent, potentially deleterious variants for analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating variant age estimates as an informative prior in a Bayesian association model will increase the statistical power to detect disease-associated genes and loci, particularly for rare non-coding variants, compared to standard burden and variance-component tests.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian rare-variant association method where the prior probability of a variant's contribution to disease risk is inversely proportional to its estimated age, derived from existing algorithms like Relate or ARGweaver applied to large biobank-scale haplotype data (e.g., UK Biobank). This age-based prior will be integrated into a hierarchical model alongside functional genomic annotations (e.g., from Ensembl VEP) within a flexible testing framework, such as an extension of the Bayesian Risk Index (BRI) model. The method will be implemented in the REGENIE/SAIGE pipeline ecosystem for scalability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source software tool for the genetics community that directly leverages population genetic history to boost RVAS power. It will generate new biological insights by identifying disease-associated genes where signal is driven by evolutionarily recent variants, potentially highlighting pathways under recent selective pressure or specific mutational mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While variant age is used in population genetics, its direct integration as a quantitative prior in association testing models is novel. This moves beyond using age as a post-hoc filter, making it a core component of the statistical model to re-weight evidence across variants.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Estimation of De Novo Mutation Rates and Ages from Deep Sequencing of Large Pedigrees
proposals.motivation: De novo mutations (DNMs) are a major source of genetic variation and disease risk, but their rates and timing are poorly resolved. Current estimates from trio sequencing are limited by statistical power and cannot distinguish mutations occurring in parental germlines from those in early embryogenesis. A precise, population-scale understanding of DNM dynamics is critical for interpreting rare variant associations in disease studies.
proposals.hypothesis: Deep, high-coverage whole-genome sequencing of large, multi-generational pedigrees will enable the accurate identification of DNMs and, through haplotype phasing and linkage disequilibrium decay analysis, allow for the estimation of their parental origin and approximate germline cell division of origin.
proposals.method: We will perform deep whole-genome sequencing (≥60x coverage) on 100 large, well-pedigreed families (minimum 3 generations, 10+ members) from diverse populations. DNMs will be called using a joint-calling pipeline (e.g., GATK HaplotypeCaller in cohort mode) with stringent filtering informed by pedigree structure. Phasing will be performed using a combination of population-based (SHAPEIT4) and pedigree-aware (MERLIN) algorithms. Mutation timing will be inferred by analyzing the length of shared haplotype segments around each DNM in descendant carriers, modeling the decay of linkage disequilibrium over meiotic generations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the most precise, population-specific estimates of DNM rates to date and pioneer a method for estimating mutation age. It will create a high-quality resource of phased DNMs for functional studies and directly inform models of genetic risk transmission, particularly for severe pediatric disorders and complex traits influenced by recent mutations.
proposals.novelty: While trio-based DNM studies are common, scaling to deep-sequenced, large pedigrees to resolve mutation timing is a novel, powerful approach. This moves beyond simply counting DNMs to reconstructing their genealogical history, a key unmet need in human genetics.

proposals.title: Dynamic Genetic Architecture of COVID-19 Severity: A Time-to-Event Genome-Wide Association Study of Clinical Progression
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for COVID-19 outcomes have identified loci associated with susceptibility and severity, but they largely assume static genetic effects. In complex diseases like cancer, genetic penetrance varies over time, influencing when disease manifests. Applying this temporal framework to COVID-19 is critical, as the genetic factors influencing initial infection may differ from those driving progression to severe outcomes like respiratory failure, with direct implications for risk stratification and therapeutic targeting.
proposals.hypothesis: The penetrance of genetic variants associated with COVID-19 is time-dependent, with distinct sets of common and rare coding variants influencing the hazard of transitioning through key clinical milestones (e.g., hospitalization, ICU admission) at different stages of disease progression.
proposals.method: We will perform a survival GWAS using large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank, COVID-19 host genetics initiative) with longitudinal clinical phenotypes. Time-to-event outcomes (time from diagnosis to hospitalization, ICU admission, or death) will be modeled using Cox proportional hazards regression, treating genetic variants (common via array, rare via exome/genome sequencing) as predictors. We will formally test for non-proportional hazards and incorporate time-dependent genetic effects. For critical intervals, we will apply time-series GWAS methods, segmenting the follow-up period to identify variants with stage-specific effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first map of time-varying genetic effects for COVID-19, moving beyond static risk scores to dynamic risk models. It will identify novel variant associations masked in case-control designs and provide mechanistic insights into the changing biological pathways driving disease progression over time.
proposals.novelty: While survival analysis is standard in oncology genetics, its application to model the dynamic penetrance of variants in an acute infectious disease is novel. This approach directly challenges the core GWAS assumption of constant effect sizes across the disease timeline.

proposals.title: A Genetics-First Framework for Clinical Trial Target Prioritization: Validating Protective Variants in SELE, ICAM5, and Coagulation Pathways
proposals.motivation: Human genetics has successfully identified risk-increasing variants for drug target discovery, but the systematic exploitation of naturally occurring protective variants to de-risk clinical development remains underutilized. Grounding therapeutic hypotheses in human evidence for resilience, rather than just disease association, could significantly improve the success rate of clinical trials by prioritizing targets with a built-in safety and efficacy profile. This is particularly urgent for complex diseases where many late-stage trials fail due to a lack of predictive human validation.
proposals.hypothesis: Druggable targets selected based on rare protective variants in genes like SELE, ICAM5, CD209, and F8 will have a higher probability of success in clinical trials, as their modulation aims to mimic a genetically-validated, protective physiological state.
proposals.method: We will first perform a meta-analysis of large-scale exome and genome sequencing data (e.g., UK Biobank, TOPMed) to identify rare coding variants with strong protective effects (P < 5e-8) against cardiovascular and inflammatory diseases. For prioritized genes, we will use in silico tools (e.g., PolyPhen-2, SIFT, AlphaFold2 for structure prediction) to characterize variant impact and infer direction of effect (loss-of-function vs. gain-of-function). We will then integrate these findings with druggable genome databases and pathway analyses to nominate specific therapeutic modalities (e.g., inhibitory antibody, agonist) for clinical trial design, directly testing the protective mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available framework for translating human protective genetics into clinical trial protocols, directly linking variant discovery to therapeutic intervention. It will deliver a prioritized shortlist of genetically-validated targets with proposed mechanisms of action for immediate clinical development, potentially setting a new standard for human-centric target selection.
proposals.novelty: While protective variants like PCSK9 and CCR5 are known, this proposes a systematic, scalable pipeline to discover and validate them as a primary filter for all clinical target prioritization, moving beyond post-hoc genetic support. It uniquely focuses on mimicking the protective genetic state as the explicit goal of drug development, not just pathway perturbation.

proposals.title: Phenome-wide interrogation of COVID-19 susceptibility loci reveals shared genetic architecture with diverse clinical phenotypes
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, large-scale genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous genetic variants associated with susceptibility to and severity of COVID-19. However, the broader phenotypic consequences of these variants remain largely unexplored. Understanding the pleiotropic effects of these risk loci is critical for predicting long-term health impacts in survivors, identifying potential drug repurposing opportunities, and elucidating the biological pathways involved in SARS-CoV-2 infection and host response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of genetic variants associated with COVID-19 outcomes will demonstrate significant pleiotropic associations with a wide range of human diseases and quantitative traits, revealing shared biological mechanisms and potential clinical comorbidities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic PheWAS of established COVID-19 risk variants (e.g., from the COVID-19 Host Genetics Initiative) across extensive electronic health record (EHR)-linked biobanks, such as the Michigan Genomics Initiative and the UK Biobank. Using a validated PheWAS pipeline, we will test associations between each variant and thousands of ICD-based phenotypes, adjusting for multiple testing. We will integrate coding and non-coding variant data, apply fine-mapping using tools like FINEMAP, and perform colocalization analyses to distinguish shared causal variants from linkage disequilibrium. Pathway enrichment will be assessed using pre-2026 databases like GO and KEGG.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive map of the pleiotropic effects of COVID-19 genetic risk factors, identifying specific clinical phenotypes that share genetic etiology with severe infection. It will generate novel hypotheses about the long-term health trajectories of individuals based on their genetic risk profile for COVID-19 and highlight potential therapeutic targets relevant to both infectious and non-communicable diseases.
proposals.novelty: While PheWAS is a established method, its application to systematically decode the clinical pleiotropy of pandemic-related genetic risk factors is novel. This moves beyond identifying risk variants to functionally contextualizing them within the broader human phenome, a crucial post-GWAS step not yet undertaken for COVID-19 loci at this scale.

proposals.title: A Family-Based Exome Sequencing Study to Identify Rare Coding Variants with Large Effects on Complex Disease Risk
proposals.motivation: Rare variant association studies (RVAS) in population cohorts are often underpowered and confounded by population stratification, limiting the discovery of high-impact genetic factors. Family-based designs, which are robust to stratification, have been underutilized in the exome sequencing era for complex traits. This approach is critical for identifying rare, penetrant variants that segregate with disease within families, offering a direct path to functional validation and therapeutic target discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a within-family RVAS, analyzing the segregation of rare coding variants in multiplex families, will identify novel, large-effect risk alleles for common complex diseases that are missed by population-based association studies.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-exome sequencing on multiplex families (≥2 affected siblings) from large biobanks like UK Biobank. We will apply the RareIBD method to identify segments identical-by-descent (IBD) and perform within-family burden and transmission disequilibrium tests (TDT) for rare (MAF<0.1%), predicted deleterious coding variants. Analyses will be stratified by variant functional consequence using pre-2026 annotation tools (e.g., CADD, MPC) and integrated with proteomics-informed variant effect predictors to prioritize candidates.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a catalog of rare, family-specific risk variants with strong genetic evidence, directly controlling for population structure. It will provide a framework for powerful family-based RVAS in biobank-scale data and generate high-confidence candidate genes for functional follow-up, bridging the gap between rare variant discovery and biological mechanism.
proposals.novelty: While family designs are classic, their application to large-scale exome sequencing data for complex trait RVAS is novel. The integration of IBD mapping with state-of-the-art variant annotation and burden testing within families represents a significant methodological advance over standard case-control RVAS.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic Atlas of Critical Illness: Integrating Whole-Genome Sequencing with Proteomics and Metabolomics to Uncover Genetic Determinants of Severity and Outcome
proposals.motivation: Critical illness, such as sepsis or acute respiratory distress syndrome, exhibits substantial heterogeneity in patient outcomes, with genetic factors playing a poorly understood role. Current diagnostic yields from exome sequencing in critically ill adults are significant (~24%) but incomplete, and rare variant association studies (RVAS) in this context lack power and mechanistic insight. Pre-2026, large-scale biobanks have demonstrated the value of integrating genomics with proteomics, but these approaches have not been applied at scale to deeply phenotyped, acute critical illness cohorts.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic integration of whole-genome sequencing with plasma proteomic and metabolomic profiling in a large, diverse critical illness cohort will identify novel, high-impact rare coding variants and polygenic risk scores that modulate disease severity and recovery through specific molecular pathways.
proposals.method: We will analyze whole-genome sequencing data from >10,000 critically ill adults (e.g., from BioVU, UK Biobank-linked ICU records) paired with Olink or SomaScan proteomic panels and untargeted metabolomics (LC-MS). We will perform RVAS for each omics layer (protein/ metabolite QTLs) and critical illness phenotypes (e.g., SOFA score, 28-day mortality) using rare variant burden tests (SKAT-O) and gene-based collapsing models. Integration will use Mendelian Randomization and multi-trait association tests (e.g., MTAG) to infer causal directions and shared genetic architecture. Proteomics-informed variant annotation, building on our prior LLM work, will prioritize functional variants.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first large-scale multi-omic map of critical illness, identifying novel genetic risk factors and their intermediate molecular phenotypes. It will deliver polygenic predictors of ICU outcomes and nominate actionable drug targets by linking genetic variants to perturbed plasma protein levels. The resource will directly address disparities by ensuring diverse cohort representation.
proposals.novelty: While multi-omics integration is established in population cohorts, its application to a large, acute critical illness cohort with detailed temporal phenotyping is novel. The proposal uniquely focuses on leveraging rare variant associations across multiple molecular layers to decipher the pathophysiology of acute, life-threatening states.

proposals.title: Refining the Clinical Actionability of Monogenic Variants by Integrating Polygenic Risk Scores and Environmental Exposures in a Large Population Cohort
proposals.motivation: Current clinical genetics often reports a ~3% actionable variant rate from exome/genome sequencing, based on high-penetrance monogenic variants. This binary classification ignores the substantial modifying effects of an individual's polygenic background and environmental context, potentially misclassifying risk and missing opportunities for stratified prevention. In the pre-2026 era of large biobanks and mature PRS methods, a more integrated, context-aware framework is needed to move from variant-centric to person-centric risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The clinical actionability and penetrance of putatively pathogenic monogenic variants for common diseases are significantly modulated by an individual's polygenic risk score and key environmental exposures, meaning that a substantial fraction of 'actionable' findings may only confer risk in specific genetic and environmental contexts.
proposals.method: We will analyze whole-exome/genome and phenotypic data from ~500k UK Biobank participants. For carriers of established pathogenic/likely pathogenic (P/LP) variants in ACMG-defined actionable genes (e.g., for cardiovascular disease, cancer), we will calculate disease-specific polygenic risk scores (PRS) using LDpred2 or PRS-CS. We will integrate self-reported and linked environmental data (e.g., smoking, BMI). Using Cox proportional hazards models with interaction terms, we will test if the hazard ratio of the P/LP variant depends on PRS percentile and environmental strata. We will develop a revised 'contextualized actionable rate' using risk reclassification metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, quantitative estimates of how polygenic background and environment modulate monogenic risk, directly challenging the static 3% actionable rate. It will deliver a practical framework for contextualizing genetic findings in clinical reports and research, improving risk stratification and targeting of preventive interventions. The results will inform guidelines for reporting secondary findings and population screening.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined gene-environment or gene-PRS interactions in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates all three layers—rare variant, polygenic score, and environment—to systematically re-define clinical actionability at a population scale, moving beyond a fixed variant list to a dynamic risk model.

proposals.title: Systematic Phenome-Wide Association Study of Loss-of-Function Intolerant Genes Lacking Disease Annotation in the UK Biobank
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide constraint metrics, such as the pLI score from gnomAD, have identified ~1,150 genes highly intolerant to loss-of-function (LOF) variants, indicating strong purifying selection and a high probability of being essential. However, a significant subset of these genes currently lacks established Mendelian disease annotation, representing a critical gap in our understanding of human genetic disease. Systematically investigating the phenotypic consequences of rare variants in these genes in a large, deeply phenotyped population is a crucial step towards novel disease gene discovery and understanding human biology.
proposals.hypothesis: Rare, predicted loss-of-function (pLOF) variants in LOF-intolerant genes without current disease annotation are associated with specific, measurable clinical phenotypes and quantitative traits in the UK Biobank population.
proposals.method: We will perform a gene-based burden test phenome-wide association study (PheWAS) in the full UK Biobank cohort (~500,000 individuals). For each of the 1,153 target genes, we will aggregate rare (MAF < 0.1%) pLOF variants and test for association across ~10,000 binary and quantitative traits derived from health records, questionnaires, and imaging. Analyses will be performed using SAIGE-GENE, a state-of-the-art method for rare-variant association testing that accounts for case-control imbalance and relatedness. We will apply stringent significance thresholds corrected for the number of genes and traits tested, and will validate top hits using exome sequencing data from independent cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly link a large set of evolutionarily constrained but poorly characterized genes to specific human phenotypes, providing strong candidates for novel Mendelian disorders and clarifying the biological role of essential genes. It will generate a public resource of gene-phenotype associations to guide clinical interpretation of variants in these genes and prioritize targets for functional follow-up.
proposals.novelty: While constraint metrics are widely used, a systematic, phenome-wide screen focused exclusively on the subset of LOF-intolerant genes *without* prior disease links, leveraging the scale and depth of the UK Biobank, has not been comprehensively performed. This moves beyond cataloging constraint to actively assigning biological and clinical meaning to it.

proposals.title: Systematic Phenotypic Characterization of Biallelic Predicted Loss-of-Function Carriers in Underexplored Genes from Large-Scale Biobanks
proposals.motivation: Large-scale exome and genome sequencing in biobanks like the UK Biobank has identified thousands of genes with rare predicted loss-of-function (pLOF) variants, but the phenotypic consequences for most remain unknown. For the majority of these newly reported genes, the impact of complete gene disruption (biallelic pLOF) has not been systematically assessed in human populations, representing a major gap in functional annotation and Mendelian disease discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, phenome-wide association study (PheWAS) of individuals carrying rare biallelic pLOF variants in these underexplored genes will reveal novel, severe Mendelian phenotypes and expand the catalog of human gene-disease relationships.
proposals.method: We will leverage exome/genome data from ~500,000 UK Biobank participants to identify carriers of biallelic rare (MAF<0.1%) pLOF variants in the 1,751 target genes. We will perform a high-throughput PheWAS using extensive clinical data (ICD-10 codes, hospital episodes, self-report, lab measures). Association testing will use burden tests (e.g., Firth logistic regression) for binary traits and linear mixed models for quantitative traits, adjusting for covariates. Significant associations will be validated using independent cohorts (e.g., FinnGen, All of Us) and explored via electronic health record review for detailed phenotypic characterization.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly link hundreds of genes of unknown function to specific human clinical phenotypes, providing essential evidence for gene-disease validity. It will generate a public resource of biallelic carrier phenotypes to guide clinical interpretation of rare variants and prioritize genes for functional follow-up. The findings will enhance our understanding of human gene essentiality and the spectrum of recessive genetic disorders.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on heterozygous pLOF carriers or known Mendelian genes, this proposal systematically targets biallelic carriers in a large, novel gene set within a deeply phenotyped population cohort, a powerful design for discovering severe recessive disorders that are under-ascertained in clinical settings.

proposals.title: Leveraging a Unified Coding Variant Catalogue with Longitudinal EHR Data for Enhanced Risk Prediction and Therapeutic Target Discovery
proposals.motivation: Large-scale exome and genome sequencing has generated extensive catalogues of coding variants, but their clinical interpretation remains a major bottleneck. The power of rare variant association studies (RVAS) is limited by small sample sizes and incomplete phenotypic characterization. Integrating these variant resources with deep, longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs) from diverse biobanks can bridge this gap, enabling the discovery of variant-phenotype associations with high temporal resolution and clinical relevance for precision medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically linking a comprehensive, population-scale coding variant catalogue to rich, longitudinal EHR data will significantly improve the statistical power for rare variant association testing, reveal novel genotype-phenotype relationships, and identify genetic modifiers of disease progression and drug response.
proposals.method: We will integrate the UK Biobank exome sequence data (or a similar large-scale resource like the Michigan Genomics Initiative) with linked EHRs. Using established RVAS frameworks (e.g., SAIGE-GENE, STAAR) and burden/sequence kernel association tests, we will test for associations between rare coding variants (aggregated by gene and predicted functional consequence) and thousands of quantitative traits, disease diagnoses, and medication usage patterns derived from EHRs. We will employ time-to-event analyses (Cox models) to assess genetic effects on disease progression and comorbidity networks. Novel associations will be validated in independent cohorts like FinnGen or All of Us.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly accessible map of rare coding variant associations with detailed clinical trajectories, moving beyond binary disease status. It will identify high-confidence genes for follow-up functional studies and potential drug targets. The framework will demonstrate a scalable model for using real-world health data to augment genetic discovery and improve polygenic risk scores by incorporating rare variant effects.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links genetics to EHRs, this proposal focuses specifically on leveraging a pre-computed, high-quality *catalogue* of coding variants for systematic, large-scale RVAS across the full phenome, with an emphasis on longitudinal outcomes and therapeutic relevance rather than single-disease discovery.

proposals.title: Genome-wide maps of non-coding constraint from large-scale whole-genome sequencing identify functional elements and enhance rare variant association studies
proposals.motivation: Current catalogs of genetic constraint, such as gnomAD, are primarily derived from exome sequencing and powerfully identify essential protein-coding genes. However, the vast majority of disease-associated variants from genome-wide association studies (GWAS) lie in non-coding regions, where systematic maps of evolutionary and mutational constraint are lacking. Generating a whole-genome constraint map is critical for interpreting non-coding variation in rare disease diagnostics and complex trait genetics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that aggregating rare variant data from whole-genome sequencing (WGS) of large, diverse population cohorts will reveal statistically robust, fine-mapped signatures of purifying selection across non-coding functional elements, including regulatory regions and non-coding RNAs, and that these constraint scores will improve the power and prioritization of rare non-coding variants in association studies.
proposals.method: We will apply the LOFTEE (Loss-Of-Function Transcript Effect Estimator) framework, extended and adapted for non-coding regions, to high-quality WGS data from cohorts like the UK Biobank and TOPMed. We will develop a novel, genome-wide constraint metric by calculating observed/expected ratios of rare variants (MAF<0.1%), stratified by functional annotation (e.g., promoter, enhancer, CTCF site) and sequence context, using mutational models from pre-2026 work. Association testing of rare non-coding variants against quantitative traits will be performed using SAIGE-GENE, integrating the new constraint scores as functional priors to boost power.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, publicly available map of non-coding constraint for the human genome, directly analogous to pLI for genes. It will provide a foundational resource for prioritizing pathogenic non-coding variants in clinical sequencing and will establish a methodological framework for incorporating functional constraint into rare variant association studies beyond the exome.
proposals.novelty: While constraint in coding regions is well-characterized, scaling this to the whole genome requires novel statistical frameworks to handle the lower signal-to-noise ratio and diverse functional annotations of non-coding DNA, moving beyond simple conservation scores to a population genetics-based metric derived directly from human WGS.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Deep Phenotyping of Carriers of Rare, Actionable Coding Variants to Elucidate Penetrance, Modifiers, and Early Disease Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies and exome sequencing have identified numerous rare, putatively pathogenic coding variants, but their clinical impact often remains uncertain due to variable penetrance and a lack of longitudinal data. This knowledge gap hinders the translation of genetic findings into precise risk prediction and preventive care, especially for actionable variants in genes like those involved in thrombophilia (e.g., F8, VWF) or other Mendelian conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic, longitudinal deep phenotyping of carriers of specific rare, actionable variants will reveal distinct subclinical trajectories, identify genetic and environmental modifiers of penetrance, and define biomarkers for early disease manifestation.
proposals.method: We will recruit a deeply phenotyped cohort of 500 carriers of prioritized rare (MAF<0.1%), actionable coding variants (e.g., in cardiovascular, metabolic, or cancer predisposition genes) identified from large biobanks like the UK Biobank and Michigan Genomics Initiative. Participants will undergo annual assessments including multi-omics profiling (plasma proteomics via Olink, metabolomics), advanced imaging (cardiac MRI, DEXA), continuous sensor monitoring, and detailed clinical evaluation. We will apply mixed-effects models to track phenotypic evolution and use rare-variant association testing within the carrier cohort to identify modifier variants influencing expressivity.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate an unprecedented longitudinal resource linking rare variant genotypes to detailed phenotypic evolution, directly quantifying penetrance and informing clinical management guidelines. It will move beyond static risk estimates to a dynamic model of disease development, identifying modifiable risk factors and early biomarkers for intervention.
proposals.novelty: While population biobanks provide scale, they lack deep, repeat phenotyping. This proposal uniquely combines the scale of biobank variant discovery with the granularity of a focused longitudinal cohort study specifically for rare variant carriers, a design not yet implemented at this depth.

proposals.title: A Scalable Framework for Detecting Epistatic Interactions Among Rare Coding Variants in Large Biobanks
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and single-variant burden tests have successfully mapped additive genetic effects for complex traits, but they largely ignore non-additive epistatic interactions, which are hypothesized to account for a portion of the missing heritability. The additive model may be insufficient for traits where biological pathways involve protein complexes or compensatory mechanisms, especially for rare, high-impact coding variants. Testing for gene-gene interactions remains a major statistical and computational challenge, particularly for rare variants where power is limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant, replicable epistatic interactions exist between genes harboring rare coding variants and that these interactions modulate risk for complex diseases more powerfully than additive single-gene burden models alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage, regression-based testing framework optimized for large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank). First, we will perform a fast, exhaustive pre-screening of all gene pairs using a modified SKAT-O statistic adapted for interaction testing. Second, top candidate pairs from the screening stage will be rigorously evaluated using a hierarchical Bayesian logistic regression model that jointly estimates interaction effects while adjusting for principal components and main effects. This model will incorporate biologically informed priors based on protein-protein interaction networks (e.g., STRING) to boost power. All analyses will leverage efficient, distributed computing pipelines built on existing tools like REGENIE and SAIGE.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a statistically robust and computationally feasible method for genome-wide epistasis screening of rare variants. It will produce a catalog of significant gene-gene interactions for a range of quantitative and dichotomous traits, providing new mechanistic insights into disease biology. The developed software will be released as an open-source extension to established rare-variant association testing toolkits.
proposals.novelty: While most rare-variant interaction studies are limited to candidate pathways, our approach performs an agnostic, genome-wide screen with a method specifically designed for the low minor allele count regime of rare coding variants. The integration of protein network priors within a scalable Bayesian testing framework is a novel synthesis of biological knowledge and statistical genetics.

proposals.title: Expanding the Phenotypic Spectrum of Rare Coding Variants: A Multi-Dimensional Phenome-Wide Association Study Using High-Throughput Clinical Biomarkers and Subclinical Imaging Endpoints
proposals.motivation: Rare variant association studies (RVAS) have successfully linked coding variants to overt clinical diagnoses, but these binary health traits may miss subtler, subclinical effects on physiology. This incomplete phenotypic mapping limits our understanding of gene function and the predictive power of genomic data for early disease risk. Leveraging the vast, multi-modal phenotypic data in large biobanks like the UK Biobank, we can now systematically test if rare variants influence a broader, continuous spectrum of biological function.
proposals.hypothesis: Rare coding variants have significant, pleiotropic associations with subclinical quantitative biomarkers and imaging-derived phenotypes that are not captured by traditional case-control disease definitions, revealing a more complete functional impact of genetic variation.
proposals.method: We will perform a phenome-wide association study (PheWAS) of rare (MAF<0.1%) predicted loss-of-function (pLoF) and deleterious missense variants in the exomes of ~500,000 UK Biobank participants. Association testing will use SAIGE-GENE or REGENIE to handle relatedness and case-control imbalance. The phenotype matrix will extend beyond ICD codes to include hundreds of quantitative blood/urine biomarkers, ECG measures, and organ-specific MRI-derived phenotypes (e.g., liver fat, heart chamber volumes, brain white matter hyperintensities). We will prioritize genes where variants show strong associations with subclinical traits but weak or no signal for related clinical diagnoses.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a comprehensive atlas linking rare variants to subclinical phenotypic effects, revealing novel gene functions and biological pathways. It will increase the power of RVAS by identifying quantitative, information-rich endpoints and provide a resource for interpreting variants of uncertain significance in a pre-clinical context.
proposals.novelty: While previous RVAS have used biomarkers, this proposal systematically integrates a uniquely broad set of high-throughput, subclinical imaging phenotypes with deep exome sequencing to dissect gene function beyond diagnosed disease, moving from a clinical to a physiological definition of trait.

proposals.title: Functional Validation of Rare Protective Variants in SLC9A3R2 and Other Novel Loci for Hypertension Using High-Throughput Cellular Assays and Exome-Wide Association Data
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide and exome-wide association studies (GWAS/ExWAS) have identified numerous loci associated with complex traits, but the vast majority of discovered variants are non-coding or of uncertain significance, and causal genes remain unconfirmed. While most focus is on risk-increasing variants, protective variants offer critical insights into disease biology and potential therapeutic targets but are rarely pursued for experimental validation. Pre-2026 large-scale biobank exome sequencing (e.g., UK Biobank) has enabled the discovery of rare coding variants with putative protective effects, such as those in SLC9A3R2 for hypertension, yet their functional mechanisms remain entirely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare coding variants in genes like SLC9A3R2, identified through exome-wide association studies as protective against hypertension, confer their effect by altering specific protein functions or pathways in relevant cell types, and that systematic functional screening can distinguish truly protective alleles from neutral variants.
proposals.method: We will first perform a meta-analysis of rare variant burden tests across multiple large cohorts (e.g., TOPMed, UK Biobank) to curate a high-confidence list of protective gene-trait associations for hypertension and related cardiovascular traits. For top candidates (starting with SLC9A3R2), we will use CRISPR-Cas9 base editing in human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived vascular smooth muscle cells and renal tubular cells to introduce the specific protective alleles identified in human data. We will employ high-throughput, multiplexed phenotypic assays measuring intracellular pH, sodium-proton exchanger activity, and contractile responses to quantify functional differences. Association between allelic series and cellular phenotypes will be assessed using mixed-effects models, directly testing the genotype-phenotype relationship.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence for the protective mechanism of SLC9A3R2 variants in hypertension, moving beyond statistical association to causal biology. It will establish a scalable framework for prioritizing and validating protective genes from population genetic data, bridging a critical gap between genetic discovery and therapeutic target identification. The validated genes and pathways will highlight new avenues for developing antihypertensive therapies that mimic natural protection.
proposals.novelty: While statistical fine-mapping of protective signals is advancing, direct experimental validation of rare protective variants in physiologically relevant human cell models is lacking. This proposal uniquely integrates large-scale genetic association data from pre-2026 resources with precise genome editing and quantitative cellular phenotyping to functionally dissect protective alleles, a strategy distinct from the dominant focus on loss-of-function or risk-increasing variants.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Rare and Coding Variant Associations with Disease Progression Trajectories in Population-Based Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully identified thousands of variants associated with disease risk, but they primarily capture cross-sectional effects on disease incidence. A critical unmet need is to understand how genetic variants, particularly rare and coding variants with potentially large functional impacts, influence the longitudinal trajectory of disease after onset, including progression rate, severity, and complication risk. This knowledge is essential for developing personalized prognostic tools and identifying therapeutic targets that modify disease course, not just initial risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare and coding genetic variants, including those identified in cross-sectional risk studies, have distinct and measurable effects on the rate and pattern of disease progression, which can be detected by analyzing longitudinal phenotypic data from deeply phenotyped population cohorts.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing large-scale longitudinal cohort data (e.g., UK Biobank, Michigan Genomics Initiative) with linked electronic health records and repeated measures. For diseases with clear progression metrics (e.g., HbA1c for diabetes, FEV1 for COPD, imaging scores for osteoarthritis), we will model individual trajectories using mixed-effects models or latent growth curve models. We will then perform rare variant association tests (e.g., SKAT-O, burden tests) and single-variant analyses within progression-relevant genes and pathways, using the derived trajectory parameters (e.g., slope, intercept) as quantitative phenotypes. Analyses will be stratified by variant class (e.g., loss-of-function, missense) and informed by prior proteomic and functional annotations.
proposals.contribution: This work will move genetic association studies from a binary risk paradigm to a dynamic model of disease, identifying variants that act as modifiers of disease severity and progression. It will generate a novel catalog of genetic factors associated with clinical heterogeneity, providing biological insights into disease mechanisms and nominating targets for drugs aimed at slowing progression. The methods developed will provide a framework for future longitudinal genetic analyses.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Abecasis and others have pioneered methods for variant discovery in cross-sectional risk, applying these to model explicit longitudinal progression phenotypes in population cohorts is a significant and under-explored advance. This integrates the power of rare variant association testing with sophisticated modeling of time-to-event and repeated-measures clinical data to answer a fundamentally different biological question.

proposals.title: Fine-Mapping Causal Rare Coding Variants by Adapting Statistical Colocalization and Credible Set Methods from GWAS
proposals.motivation: Rare variant association studies (RVAS) have successfully linked genes to complex traits, but identifying the specific causal rare variants within a gene remains a major challenge. This impedes biological interpretation, functional validation, and clinical translation. While statistical fine-mapping is a mature tool for common variants in GWAS, its direct application to rare variants is hindered by low allele frequencies, linkage disequilibrium patterns, and the need for extremely large sample sizes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting GWAS fine-mapping principles—specifically, leveraging functional annotations, variant-specific weights, and Bayesian colocalization frameworks—we can construct statistically robust credible sets of putatively causal rare variants within associated genes, even with current exome and genome sequencing cohort sizes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SuSiE (Sum of Single Effects) model and colocalization methods to the rare variant context. The approach will integrate variant-level functional priors (e.g., CADD scores, MPC, AlphaMissense predictions) to inform causal probability. We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model that jointly analyzes gene-based RVAS signals and variant-level data, using MCMC and variational inference for computation. The method will be validated using simulated data and applied to real RVAS results from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, TOPMed) for traits like thrombotic risk, where associated genes (e.g., F8, VWF) harbor multiple rare coding variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source software tool for rare variant fine-mapping, enabling researchers to prioritize specific variants for functional follow-up. It will increase the resolution of RVAS findings, moving from gene-level to variant-level associations, and provide a formal statistical framework to assess whether multiple rare variants within a gene independently contribute to disease risk.
proposals.novelty: While fine-mapping for common variants is standard, its systematic adaptation for rare coding variants is not. The novelty lies in the tailored integration of functional prediction scores as priors within a Bayesian colocalization framework specifically designed for the statistical properties of rare variant data in large-scale sequencing studies.

proposals.title: Deep Exome Sequencing of Extreme Phenotypes to Uncover Rare, High-Effect Coding Variants for Complex Traits
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully identified common variants for complex traits, but these explain only a fraction of heritability. Rare coding variants, hypothesized to have larger effect sizes, remain underexplored due to the immense sample sizes required for sufficient statistical power in population-based sequencing studies. Focusing on individuals at the extreme tails of trait distributions offers a powerful, cost-effective strategy to enrich for such variants.
proposals.hypothesis: Deep exome sequencing of individuals with extreme values of quantitative traits (e.g., blood pressure, lipid levels, BMI) will yield a significantly higher discovery rate of rare, penetrant coding variants with large phenotypic effects compared to sequencing a random population sample of the same size.
proposals.method: We will perform high-coverage (>100x) exome sequencing on ~5,000 individuals selected from the top and bottom 1% of trait distributions within large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, Michigan Genomics Initiative). Association testing will use burden and sequence kernel association tests (SKAT) optimized for rare variants, comparing allele frequencies in extreme cases to ancestry-matched controls from the middle of the trait distribution. We will integrate functional annotations from pre-2026 resources (e.g., gnomAD, CADD, AlphaMissense) to prioritize likely causal variants and apply Mendelian randomization frameworks to assess causal relationships between discovered genes and traits.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly identify novel gene-trait associations and provide a curated set of high-confidence, large-effect rare variants for functional follow-up. It will demonstrate the efficiency of extreme phenotype sampling for gene discovery and provide a scalable framework for applying deep sequencing to other complex traits within existing biobank resources.
proposals.novelty: While extreme phenotype designs are established, their application with deep exome sequencing in large, deeply phenotyped adult biobanks is not yet routine. This proposal systematically scales down sequencing to a highly informative subset, maximizing power per sequenced sample, and integrates this with robust, pre-2026 statistical genetics methods for rare variant analysis.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Shared Genetic Architecture of Infection Susceptibility and Severe Disease Progression Using Rare and Coding Variants
proposals.motivation: Understanding whether genetic risk factors for initial infection are distinct from those for disease severity is crucial for developing targeted prevention and treatment strategies. This question gained prominence during the COVID-19 pandemic, but remains unresolved for many pathogens. A systematic, large-scale genetic analysis can clarify if public health measures and therapeutic targets should differ for preventing infection versus mitigating severe outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic variants influencing susceptibility to infection are largely distinct from those influencing progression to severe disease, implying separate biological pathways for viral entry/initial immune response and for dysregulated systemic inflammation or organ damage.
proposals.method: We will perform two-stage rare variant association studies (RVAS) and genome-wide association studies (GWAS) in large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, Michigan Genomics Initiative).
