For specific infections (e.g., SARS-CoV-2, influenza, sepsis), we will define two orthogonal phenotypes: susceptibility (infected vs. unexposed controls) and severity among cases (severe hospitalization vs. mild outpatient). We will apply gene-based burden and SKAT-O tests for rare coding variants, and GWAS for common variants, using REGENIE for scalable regression. Genetic correlation (LDSC) and Mendelian Randomization will formally test for shared architecture.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of shared and distinct genetic loci for infection susceptibility and severity across multiple pathogens. It will identify novel drug targets specific to preventing severe progression and inform the utility of polygenic risk scores for stratifying patients at highest risk of complications upon infection.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies often conflate infection and severity, we explicitly model them as separate, conditionally independent phenotypes. Furthermore, we integrate rare variant analysis from exome sequencing—a strength of the Abecasis lab—with common variant GWAS to capture the full spectrum of genetic risk.

proposals.title: Systematic Detection of Epistatic Interactions Among Common Risk Variants in Polygenic Scores
proposals.motivation: Polygenic risk scores (PRS) predominantly assume additive genetic effects, potentially missing non-additive interactions (epistasis) that could explain additional disease heritability and improve risk prediction. In the pre-2026 landscape, large biobanks with genome-wide data and computational tools for interaction testing enable a focused search for epistasis among established risk loci, moving beyond genome-wide screens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, statistically significant pairwise or higher-order epistatic interactions exist between the common variants constituting established polygenic risk scores, and that modeling these interactions will improve phenotypic prediction and biological understanding.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale cohorts like the UK Biobank, focusing on a disease trait with a well-powered PRS (e.g., coronary artery disease). Using the six-SNP risk score as a scaffold, we will exhaustively test for statistical interactions (epistasis) between all SNP pairs using regression models with a multiplicative interaction term, adjusting for principal components. We will apply a variance component model (e.g., using the SKAT-O framework adapted for interaction testing) to assess the aggregate contribution of epistasis. Significant interactions will be validated in independent datasets and functionally annotated using pre-2026 regulatory genomics resources (e.g., ENCODE, GTEx).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, large-scale assessment of the prevalence and effect size of epistasis among common risk variants in a PRS context. It will deliver a methodological framework for targeted epistasis detection and, if interactions are found, an improved risk prediction model and new hypotheses about biological pathways involving non-additive genetic effects.
proposals.novelty: Instead of an underpowered genome-wide epistasis scan, we perform a targeted, hypothesis-driven search for interactions specifically within a curated set of established risk variants, significantly increasing power and biological interpretability. This bridges the gap between agnostic interaction searches and additive PRS models.

proposals.title: Age-Dependent Penetrance of a Protective ACE2 Coding Variant: A Large-Scale Rare Variant Association Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 genetic association studies often assume genetic effects are constant across the lifespan, but this may not hold for variants influencing age-related diseases like cardiovascular or respiratory conditions. The ACE2 gene, encoding the SARS-CoV-2 entry receptor, harbors rare coding variants with reported protective effects against severe COVID-19. Testing if this effect is modified by age is critical for understanding disease biology and for accurate risk prediction in different demographic groups.
proposals.hypothesis: The protective effect of a specific, rare coding variant in the ACE2 gene on severe COVID-19 outcomes is not constant but attenuates with increasing patient age.
proposals.method: We will perform a rare variant association study stratified by age decade using exome/genome sequencing and COVID-19 outcome data from large, diverse cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank, COVID-19 host genetics initiative). We will focus on a pre-specified, putatively protective ACE2 coding variant (e.g., p.Arg514Gly). The primary analysis will use a logistic regression model with an interaction term between variant carrier status and age (continuous), adjusted for sex, genetic ancestry, and relevant comorbidities. Significance will be assessed via a likelihood ratio test comparing models with and without the interaction term.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, direct test of age-dependent genetic effects for a high-profile protective variant, moving beyond the constant-effect assumption. It will clarify the utility of this variant for risk stratification in different age groups and could inform mechanistic studies on how aging interacts with host genetics in viral infection.
proposals.novelty: While age is commonly used as a covariate, explicitly testing for genetic effect modification by age for a specific, rare coding variant in a large population-scale dataset is not standard practice in pre-2026 association studies. This approach directly challenges a fundamental, often untested, modeling assumption.

proposals.title: Cross-Ancestry Validation and Refinement of a Proteomics-Informed Genetic Risk Score for Thrombotic Disorders
proposals.motivation: Polygenic risk scores (PRS) derived from genome-wide association studies (GWAS) show immense promise for personalized medicine but have limited portability across ancestries due to differences in allele frequencies, linkage disequilibrium patterns, and causal variant architecture. This lack of generalizability exacerbates health disparities. Recent work, including Abecasis's on thrombotic risk variants, underscores the need to validate and adapt genetic risk models in diverse, large-scale cohorts to ensure equitable clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: A genetic risk score for thrombotic disorders, initially developed in a European-ancestry cohort and informed by proteomic data, will demonstrate significantly attenuated predictive power in non-European populations unless explicitly recalibrated using ancestry-specific variant weights and haplotype structures.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank, All of Us) with linked electronic health records. The initial risk score, built from variants in ABO, F8, VWF, and other loci, will be applied to individuals of African, East Asian, South Asian, and Hispanic/Latino ancestries. We will assess its predictive power for thrombotic events using Cox proportional hazards models. To improve portability, we will employ PRS-CSx, a Bayesian method for cross-population polygenic prediction that uses continuous shrinkage priors and ancestry-specific GWAS summary statistics to compute posterior mean effect sizes, and evaluate its performance against the naive transfer.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous assessment of the cross-ancestry portability of a biologically informed genetic risk score, quantifying the disparity in predictive performance. It will deliver a refined, more universally applicable risk model, directly addressing a major barrier to the clinical implementation of PRS. The findings will establish a framework for validating genetic risk tools in diverse populations.
proposals.novelty: While PRS portability is a known challenge, this work uniquely integrates a proteomics-informed variant prioritization framework with state-of-the-art cross-ancestry PRS methods (PRS-CSx) to validate and refine a specific, clinically relevant risk model for thrombosis, moving beyond theoretical evaluation to practical model improvement.

proposals.title: Cell-type-specific regulatory mechanisms of COVID-19 severity loci revealed by single-cell eQTL mapping in human respiratory epithelium and immune cells
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous loci associated with COVID-19 severity, but the causal variants, target genes, and specific cell types mediating these risks remain largely unresolved. This gap hinders the translation of genetic findings into actionable biological insights and therapeutic targets. Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) of respiratory tissues provides an unprecedented opportunity to map expression quantitative trait loci (eQTLs) with cell-type resolution, which is critical given the diverse cellular responses to SARS-CoV-2 infection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of COVID-19 GWAS risk variants exert their effects by modulating gene expression in specific cell types of the respiratory tract and lung microenvironment, and that mapping single-cell eQTLs will reveal these context-specific regulatory mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will integrate summary statistics from large-scale COVID-19 host genetics consortia (e.g., COVID-19 Host Genetics Initiative) with scRNA-seq data from respiratory tissues (e.g., nasal, bronchial, alveolar samples) from diverse cohorts, including the Human Cell Atlas and disease-specific studies. Using established single-cell eQTL mapping frameworks (e.g., tensorQTL) and mixed-effects models to account for individual and batch effects, we will identify cell-type-specific eQTLs. We will then perform colocalization and Mendelian randomization analyses to prioritize likely causal gene-cell-type pairs for functional validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive atlas of cell-type-specific regulatory effects for COVID-19 risk variants in relevant tissues, directly linking GWAS signals to causal cell types and genes. It will provide a mechanistic framework for understanding individual risk and identify high-priority cell-type-specific targets for therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: While bulk-tissue eQTL studies exist, applying rigorous single-cell eQTL mapping specifically to COVID-19 genetics in primary respiratory tissues is novel. This approach moves beyond annotation to actively dissect the cellular context of polygenic risk, a key unmet need in post-GWAS functional genomics.

proposals.title: Integrated Polygenic Risk Scores for COVID-19 Severity: Development and Clinical Validation in Diverse Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Despite the identification of host genetic factors for severe COVID-19 through large genome-wide association studies (GWAS), translating these findings into clinically actionable tools remains a challenge. Current polygenic risk scores (PRS) for COVID-19 severity are typically based on common variants from single-trait GWAS and have shown limited predictive power when integrated with clinical risk factors, hindering their utility in patient stratification and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an integrated PRS, combining common variant effects from multi-ancestry COVID-19 GWAS with functional, coding, and rare variant information from exome/genome sequencing, will significantly improve the clinical prediction of severe COVID-19 outcomes beyond standard clinical models.
proposals.method: We will develop PRS using state-of-the-art methods such as PRS-CS and LDpred2, leveraging summary statistics from the COVID-19 Host Genetics Initiative. This common variant PRS will be integrated with a burden of rare, predicted loss-of-function variants in known susceptibility genes (e.g., TLR7, IFNAR2) and functionally informed coding variants, weighted by proteomic impact scores. We will validate the integrated score in large, diverse biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, Michigan Genomics Initiative) and hospitalized patient cohorts, assessing discrimination and reclassification improvement when added to clinical models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, clinically integrated genetic risk tool for COVID-19 severity, directly addressing racial disparities in genetic prediction by focusing on multi-ancestry development. It will provide a framework for combining common and rare variant architectures for complex trait prediction, moving genetic associations towards clinical application.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of common polygenic risk with curated rare and coding variant burdens from sequencing data, moving beyond standard GWAS-based PRS, and the rigorous validation of this combined model for clinical prediction in diverse populations.

proposals.title: Multi-omic Integration of Rare Coding Variants and Molecular Phenotypes to Elucidate Heterogeneous COVID-19 Outcomes
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic revealed profound inter-individual heterogeneity in disease severity, from asymptomatic infection to critical illness. While GWAS have identified common variant associations, they explain a limited fraction of the heritable risk, particularly for severe outcomes. Integrating rare, potentially high-impact coding variants from exome/genome sequencing with proteomic, metabolomic, and epigenomic data from patient cohorts offers a powerful, yet underutilized, strategy to uncover causal pathways and molecular mechanisms driving clinical heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating rare coding variants with multi-omic intermediate phenotypes will identify novel gene-trait associations, reveal distinct biological pathways for severe COVID-19, and improve the functional interpretation of both common and rare genetic risk factors.
proposals.method: We will apply and extend rare variant association testing frameworks (e.g., SKAT-O, burden tests) to large-scale COVID-19 host genetics consortia data (e.g., COVID-19 Host Genetics Initiative). Genetic associations will be integrated with matched plasma proteomic (Olink/SomaScan), metabolomic (mass spectrometry), and epigenomic (DNA methylation array) data from patient sub-cohorts. We will employ Mendelian Randomization and colocalization analyses to infer causal relationships between molecular layers and clinical outcomes. Proteomics-informed variant effect predictors, building on our prior work, will be used to prioritize likely pathogenic rare variants for functional follow-up.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a comprehensive map of how rare genetic variation influences molecular networks perturbed in COVID-19, moving beyond single-variant associations. It will nominate high-confidence causal genes and proteins for therapeutic targeting. The developed analytical framework will serve as a blueprint for multi-omic integration in other complex diseases, enhancing the power of rare variant studies.
proposals.novelty: While multi-omic studies exist, few have systematically integrated rare coding variants at scale, especially in the context of an acute infectious disease. This proposal uniquely combines Abecasis's expertise in rare variant analysis with deep molecular phenotyping to dissect the mechanisms of a clinically urgent condition, moving from genetic association to actionable biological insight.

proposals.title: Cross-Ancestry Replication and Discovery of Sex-Dimorphic Genetic Loci for Cardiometabolic Traits
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified sex-dimorphic loci, such as IRS1 and ZNF12, influencing cardiometabolic traits, but these findings are overwhelmingly derived from European-ancestry cohorts. This limits their clinical utility and biological understanding, as allele frequencies, linkage disequilibrium patterns, and genetic effects can differ substantially across ancestries, potentially obscuring key population-specific or shared genetic architectures of sex differences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of known sex-dimorphic loci will replicate in non-European populations, but that ancestry-specific analyses will also reveal novel, population-enriched loci and improve fine-mapping resolution at shared signals.
proposals.method: We will perform sex-stratified and sex-interaction GWAS for a panel of cardiometabolic traits (e.g., lipid levels, glycemic traits, BMI) in large-scale biobanks with diverse ancestry, including the UK Biobank, All of Us, and PAGE consortium data. Analyses will use established GWAS pipelines (e.g., REGENIE, SAIGE) to handle relatedness and binary traits. We will assess replication of known loci (like IRS1) via trans-ancestry meta-analysis and ancestry-specific discovery. Fine-mapping will be conducted using SuSiE, integrating ancestry-specific LD reference panels from the 1000 Genomes Project to improve resolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly assess the generalizability of a key class of genetic findings—sex-dimorphic effects—across global populations. It will produce a catalog of replicated and novel loci, highlighting which biological pathways are conserved and which are population-specific, thereby advancing equitable genetic discovery and informing the design of more inclusive genetic risk scores.
proposals.novelty: While cross-ancestry GWAS replication is established, a focused, large-scale investigation specifically targeting the portability of *sex-dimorphic* genetic effects remains limited. This study systematically addresses this gap, leveraging Abecasis's expertise in large-scale variant association and rare variant analysis to dissect complex trait architecture in diverse cohorts.

proposals.title: Colocalization of Sex-Dimorphic GWAS Signals with Tissue-Specific eQTLs to Identify Causal Regulatory Variants
proposals.motivation: Many complex traits exhibit significant sex differences in prevalence or severity, yet the molecular mechanisms underlying these disparities remain poorly understood. While genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified sex-dimorphic loci, distinguishing the causal regulatory variants from linked non-functional variants is a major challenge, limiting biological insight and therapeutic target discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of sex-dimorphic GWAS signals are driven by regulatory variants whose effects on gene expression are shared across sexes but have differential phenotypic impact, and that these causal variants can be identified by colocalizing GWAS signals with sex-stratified expression quantitative trait loci (eQTLs) in relevant tissues.
proposals.method: We will apply rigorous statistical colocalization methods, specifically the Bayesian COLOC framework and the fastENLOC probabilistic model, to large-scale, sex-stratified GWAS summary statistics (e.g., for autoimmune, cardiovascular, and metabolic traits) and sex-stratified eQTL data from consortia like GTEx and eQTLGen. We will prioritize colocalized signals with high posterior probability (PP4 > 0.8) and then functionally annotate the putative causal variants using coding and regulatory predictions from tools like ANNOVAR and CADD, and validate associations in independent cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a prioritized list of high-confidence causal regulatory variants underlying sex differences in complex traits. It will provide a functional interpretation for sex-dimorphic GWAS loci and nominate specific genes and regulatory mechanisms for experimental follow-up, moving beyond association to causality.
proposals.novelty: While colocalization is established, its systematic application to sex-stratified GWAS and eQTL data to dissect the genetic architecture of sex-biased traits is novel. This approach directly tests whether shared causal variants, rather than distinct ones, can explain phenotypic differences via context-dependent effects.

proposals.title: Fine-mapping the 15q25 locus for lung cancer risk using deep-coverage reference panels to impute and test rare coding variants
proposals.motivation: The 15q25 locus is a major GWAS hotspot for lung cancer risk, but fine-mapping has been limited by the inability of standard genotyping arrays and reference panels (e.g., 1000 Genomes, Haplotype Reference Consortium) to accurately impute rare and low-frequency coding variants. Emerging large-scale, whole-genome sequencing (WGS) reference panels, such as the TOPMed and UK Biobank WGS resources, provide denser haplotype information, enabling improved imputation of rare variants down to ~0.01% allele frequency. This presents a critical opportunity to resolve the specific causal variants, particularly in genes like CHRNA5, which may inform biological mechanisms and therapeutic targeting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that leveraging deep-coverage WGS reference panels will enable the imputation of rare, potentially functional variants at the 15q25 locus, and that these variants will explain a significant portion of the heritability and refine the set of putative causal variants beyond those identified by common SNP associations.
proposals.method: We will apply the TOPMed Imputation Server (or the Michigan Imputation Server with a TOPMed reference panel) to impute genotypes in large, existing lung cancer GWAS consortia datasets (e.g., ILCOC, OncoArray). We will focus on the 15q25 region, using the rare variant association testing framework in REGENIE or SAIGE, which is robust to case-control imbalance, to perform gene-based burden and SKAT tests on imputed rare (MAF<0.01) coding variants. Fine-mapping will be conducted using statistical methods like SuSiE (Sum of Single Effects model) that account for allelic heterogeneity and leverage the improved correlation structure from the denser panel.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a refined set of high-probability causal variants at 15q25, potentially identifying specific rare coding changes that drive lung cancer risk. It will demonstrate a practical framework for using next-generation reference panels to augment existing GWAS data for rare variant fine-mapping. The results will move the field from broad association signals towards specific, potentially actionable genetic mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While using TOPMed for imputation is established, its systematic application to fine-map a classic GWAS locus for rare coding variants, combined with state-of-the-art rare variant association and fine-mapping methods, represents a novel integration of resources to solve a long-standing problem in cancer genetics.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Interaction Analysis of Rare Coding Variants and Smoking Exposure at the 15q25 Nicotinic Receptor Locus in Lung Cancer Risk
proposals.motivation: The 15q25 locus, harboring CHRNA5, CHRNA3, and CHRNB4, is the strongest genetic signal for smoking behavior and lung cancer risk identified in genome-wide association studies (GWAS). However, the independent and joint effects of rare coding variants within these genes, and how they modulate the impact of environmental smoking exposure, remain poorly characterized, limiting biological insight and personalized risk prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare, functional variants in CHRNA5, CHRNA3, and CHRNB4 interact synergistically with each other (gene-gene) and multiplicatively with smoking dose and duration (gene-environment) to define distinct, high-risk profiles for nicotine dependence and lung adenocarcinoma.
proposals.method: We will perform a large-scale, phenome-wide analysis using exome/genome sequencing data from cohorts like UK Biobank and TOPMed, focusing on rare (MAF<0.01) coding variants in the three target genes. We will apply statistical methods for rare-variant association testing (e.g., SKAT-O, burden tests) and explicitly model gene-gene and gene-environment interactions using regression frameworks with penalization (LASSO) to handle high-dimensional terms. Environmental exposure will be quantified via detailed smoking history variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the combinatorial architecture of risk at a canonical GWAS locus, moving beyond single-variant signals. It will provide a model for integrating rare variant data with environmental exposures to explain missing heritability and identify high-risk subgroups, directly informing targeted prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While the locus is well-known, a systematic investigation of rare variant interactions within the locus and with smoking metrics using large-scale sequencing data is novel, moving from broad association to mechanistic interaction networks.

proposals.title: Integrating Fine-Mapped GWAS Variants with Brain eQTLs to Elucidate Regulatory Mechanisms in Neuropsychiatric and Neurological Disorders
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of non-coding variants associated with neuropsychiatric and neurological traits, but linking these signals to causal genes and mechanisms remains a major challenge. Expression quantitative trait loci (eQTL) data from post-mortem human brain tissues, such as those from the GTEx and PsychENCODE consortia (pre-2026), provide a critical but underutilized resource to bridge this gap and pinpoint the regulatory pathways through which these variants act.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fine-mapped, high-probability causal SNPs from large-scale GWAS for disorders like schizophrenia, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease will be significantly enriched for brain tissue-specific eQTLs, and that this integration will identify specific regulatory genes and cell-type contexts underlying disease risk.
proposals.method: We will compile fine-mapped variant sets from pre-2026 GWAS meta-analyses (e.g., from the UK Biobank, PGC) and overlap them with eQTL summary statistics from brain tissues (cortex, cerebellum, etc.) from GTEx v8 and PsychENCODE. Statistical colocalization will be performed using established methods (e.g., COLOC, eCAVIAR) to assign posterior probabilities for shared causal variants. We will further integrate single-nucleus RNA-seq data (where available) to resolve cell-type-specific regulatory effects and use functional genomic annotations (e.g., chromatin accessibility) to validate putative mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a prioritized list of likely causal gene-regulator pairs for major brain disorders, moving beyond association to mechanism. It will provide a public resource of integrated brain variant-gene maps to guide experimental validation and highlight tissue- and cell-type-specific regulatory architectures that could inform therapeutic targeting.
proposals.novelty: While eQTL colocalization is established, this proposal focuses systematically on fine-mapped SNPs across multiple brain disorders and diverse brain tissues, leveraging the latest pre-2026 datasets and colocalization methods to achieve higher resolution and specificity than previous broad-overlap studies.

proposals.title: A Multi-Ancestry Genome-Wide Association Study of Body Fat Distribution to Improve Polygenic Risk Prediction and Locus Discovery
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for body fat distribution, a key determinant of cardiometabolic risk, have been overwhelmingly conducted in European-ancestry populations. This severely limits the generalizability of findings and the portability of polygenic risk scores (PRS) to non-European groups, exacerbating health disparities. Expanding large-scale genetic studies in diverse ancestries is a critical, pre-2026 priority to ensure equitable benefits from genomic medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conducting a large-scale, well-powered GWAS for fat distribution phenotypes (e.g., waist-to-hip ratio adjusted for BMI) in diverse populations will identify novel ancestry-specific and shared genetic loci, and that a multi-ancestry meta-analysis will significantly improve the trans-ancestry portability of derived PRS.
proposals.method: We will perform GWAS in large, ancestrally diverse cohorts (e.g., African, Hispanic/Latino, East Asian, South Asian) using standardized fat distribution phenotypes from imaging and anthropometry. Analyses will leverage established, pre-2026 GWAS pipelines (e.g., REGENIE, SAIGE) to handle population structure and relatedness. We will conduct ancestry-specific GWAS followed by fixed-effects, multi-ancestry meta-analysis using METAL. Fine-mapping and heritability estimation will be performed using methods like Sum of Single Effects (SuSiE) and LD Score regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the largest multi-ancestry GWAS resource for body fat distribution to date, directly addressing a major gap in the field. It will yield novel genetic associations, improve the biological understanding of adiposity, and generate PRS with demonstrably better performance across global populations, moving towards more equitable genetic risk prediction.
proposals.novelty: While the need for diversity in genomics is recognized, this proposal systematically addresses it for the clinically important trait of fat distribution by executing a coordinated, large-scale analysis across multiple understudied populations, moving beyond cataloging disparities to actively generating the foundational data required to resolve them.

proposals.title: Dual-Trait Burden Analysis of Rare Coding Variants Reveals Distinct Genetic Architectures for Protective and Risk Fat Distribution Phenotypes
proposals.motivation: Body fat distribution is a major determinant of cardiometabolic risk, independent of overall adiposity. While genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified common variants associated with adverse fat distribution patterns (e.g., higher waist-to-hip ratio), the role of rare coding variants in both predisposing to unfavorable patterns and conferring protection for favorable patterns remains poorly characterized. A dual analysis could reveal novel therapeutic targets by identifying genes where loss-of-function variants are not just neutral but actively beneficial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare, predicted loss-of-function (pLOF) and deleterious missense variants will aggregate in distinct sets of genes for individuals with extreme unfavorable (high-risk) versus favorable (protective) fat distribution phenotypes, and that the protective gene set will be enriched for pathways distinct from simply the inverse of risk pathways.
proposals.method: We will perform a dual-trait burden analysis in large, deeply-phenotyped cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank, Michigan Genomics Initiative) with exome/genome sequencing and DXA-derived body composition metrics. Participants will be stratified into extreme deciles for protective (e.g., low visceral, high gluteofemoral fat) and risk (high visceral, low gluteofemoral fat) phenotypes. We will apply gene-based collapsing tests (e.g., SKAT-O, Firth's logistic regression) separately in each extreme group versus the middle 80% of the population, focusing on rare (MAF<0.1%) pLOF and deleterious (CADD>30) coding variants. Pathway and network analyses (e.g., DEPICT, MAGMA) will be conducted on significant gene sets.
proposals.contribution: This study will 1) identify novel genes harboring rare variants that confer protection against adverse fat distribution, a largely unexplored area with high therapeutic potential, and 2) provide a more complete genetic model of body composition by explicitly modeling both tails of the phenotypic distribution, moving beyond a single-disease risk framework. The results will prioritize genes for functional validation as potential targets for mimicking protective genetics.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard case-control burden studies that treat one extreme as 'cases', this proposal explicitly defines and analyzes two distinct extreme phenotypic groups (protective and risk) as separate 'cases', enabling the discovery of genetic mechanisms specific to a healthy metabolic phenotype that are not merely the absence of risk variants.

proposals.title: Dissecting the independence and interaction of genetic risk loci and environmental exposures in primary biliary cholangitis etiology
proposals.motivation: Primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) has strong genetic risk loci identified through GWAS and rare variant studies, yet its incomplete penetrance and female predominance suggest critical roles for environmental triggers like smoking, urinary tract infections, and chemical exposures. Pre-2026, most studies analyze genetic and environmental factors separately, leaving a key gap in understanding whether genetic risk acts independently or requires specific environmental co-factors to trigger disease. This limits mechanistic insight and personalized risk prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that established PBC genetic risk loci (e.g., in HLA, IL12A, IL12RB2) exhibit effect-size heterogeneity across strata of specific environmental exposures, and that a subset of loci interact synergistically with exposures to confer disease risk, while others act independently.
proposals.method: We will perform a genome-environment interaction study (GxE) in a large, deeply phenotyped PBC case-control cohort (e.g., UK Biobank, PBC Genetics Consortium) with harmonized environmental data. We will test for GxE using a two-step approach: first, a marginal screening of GWAS loci for effect heterogeneity across exposure strata using modified Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel tests; second, a focused interaction test for top loci using a logistic regression framework with a multiplicative interaction term, adjusting for principal components and covariates. Rare coding variants from exome sequencing will be aggregated into gene-based burdens and tested for GxE using analogous methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate which genetic risks for PBC are constitutive versus contingent on environment, directly informing disease mechanisms. It will generate a prioritized list of gene-environment pairs for functional validation and refine risk models by incorporating interaction terms, moving beyond additive genetic risk scores.
proposals.novelty: While GxE is common in complex traits, a systematic, large-scale interaction analysis focused specifically on the well-powered genetic architecture of PBC, integrating both common GWAS signals and rare coding variants from exome data, is novel and leverages Abecasis's expertise in variant association methods.

proposals.title: Discovery of Population-Specific and Trans-Ancestry Risk Loci via Large-Scale Genome-Wide Association Studies in African, Hispanic/Latino, and Indigenous American Cohorts
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, the overwhelming majority of genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have been conducted in European and, to a lesser extent, East Asian ancestries, creating a significant bias in genetic discovery and limiting the generalizability of polygenic risk scores. This lack of diversity hinders the identification of population-specific risk variants and the fine-mapping of causal loci, which is crucial for equitable precision medicine. Recent work, including studies on diagnostic disparities, underscores the urgent need to expand genetic resources in underrepresented groups.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conducting large-scale GWAS in ancestrally diverse, non-European and non-Asian cohorts will reveal novel, population-specific risk loci for common diseases and improve the portability of genetic risk prediction across global populations.
proposals.method: We will aggregate and harmonize genetic data from existing and newly recruited cohorts of African, Hispanic/Latino, and Indigenous American ancestry, targeting a minimum sample size of 100,000 individuals per group. Association testing for a panel of cardiometabolic and autoimmune traits will be performed using REGENIE for efficient mixed-model analysis, accounting for relatedness and population structure. We will integrate exome sequencing data to assess the contribution of rare coding variants, applying burden tests and SKAT-O. Trans-ancestry meta-analysis will be conducted using MR-MEGA to distinguish population-specific from shared risk loci.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, disease-focused GWAS summary statistics for several critically underrepresented populations, directly enabling the development of more equitable polygenic risk scores. It will identify novel risk loci with potential population-specific effect sizes or allele frequencies, enhancing biological understanding of disease etiology. The resource will also improve fine-mapping resolution for known loci by leveraging diverse linkage disequilibrium patterns.
proposals.novelty: While the need for diversity in genomics is acknowledged, this proposal systematically executes a large-scale, multi-trait analysis specifically focused on groups with the most severe underrepresentation (e.g., Indigenous Americans), and integrates common variant GWAS with rare variant analysis from exome data within the same cohorts, a combination not yet achieved at this scale.

proposals.title: Integrating Rare and Common Variant Association Studies with Deep Phenotyping to Elucidate Genetic Modifiers of Primary Biliary Cholangitis Severity and Ursodeoxycholic Acid Response
proposals.motivation: Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC) has a strong genetic component, but known GWAS loci primarily explain disease susceptibility, not the highly variable clinical course and treatment response. This heterogeneity leads to unpredictable outcomes and suboptimal management. Integrating detailed, longitudinal clinical phenotypes with comprehensive genetic data, including rare coding variants, is a critical pre-2026 need to move from risk prediction to mechanistic understanding of disease progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating common variant polygenic risk scores with burden tests of rare, predicted-deleterious coding variants in biologically relevant pathways will identify novel genetic modifiers that correlate with PBC disease severity stages and differential response to first-line therapy with ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA).
proposals.method: We will perform a two-stage genetic association study in a deeply phenotyped PBC cohort. First, we will generate exome sequencing and genome-wide array data. For rare variants, we will apply gene- and pathway-based burden tests using optimized variant annotation pipelines (e.g., integrating CADD, REVEL scores). For common variants, we will calculate a PBC-specific polygenic risk score (PRS). We will then employ multivariate regression and machine learning models (e.g., penalized regression, random forests) to correlate these genetic features with quantitative phenotypes of disease progression (e.g., ALP/ bilirubin trajectories, fibrosis scores) and binary UDCA response, adjusting for key covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, integrated genetic model for PBC prognosis, identifying specific variant classes and pathways that modulate clinical severity. It will provide a framework for stratifying patients at diagnosis based on genetic likelihood of progression or treatment failure, informing more personalized monitoring and therapeutic strategies. The findings will also highlight new biological targets for drug development in treatment-refractory disease.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have identified PBC risk loci, this proposal uniquely focuses on severity and treatment response by jointly modeling the contributions of rare coding variants—often missed by GWAS—and common variant PRS within a deep phenotyping context, a synthesis not yet comprehensively applied in PBC.

proposals.title: Exome Sequencing and Rare Variant Burden Analysis in Familial Primary Biliary Cholangitis to Identify High-Penetrance Risk Genes
proposals.motivation: Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC) has a strong but incompletely understood genetic component. While common variant GWAS have identified numerous risk loci, these explain only a fraction of familial risk, suggesting a role for rare, high-penetrance variants. Studying multiplex PBC families provides a powerful, yet underutilized, design to discover such variants and elucidate the monogenic or oligogenic architecture underlying severe, familial disease forms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare, coding variants with moderate-to-high penetrance, which are not captured by GWAS, segregate within multiplex PBC families and can be identified through exome sequencing and family-based burden analyses.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-exome sequencing on probands from 100+ multiplex PBC families (≥2 affected first-degree relatives). Variant calling and quality control will follow established pipelines (e.g., GATK best practices). We will apply family-based rare variant association tests (e.g., pedigree-based burden tests, FAMSEQ) to identify genes with an excess of rare, predicted-damaging variants segregating with disease. Candidate genes will be functionally annotated using pre-2026 databases (gnomAD, ClinVar) and in silico predictors (e.g., CADD, PolyPhen-2).
proposals.contribution: This work will identify novel, high-penetrance risk genes for PBC, providing new biological insights into disease pathogenesis. It will deliver a catalog of rare variants with direct clinical relevance for genetic testing in high-risk families and establish a framework for applying family-based designs to other complex diseases with familial aggregation.
proposals.novelty: This proposal shifts focus from population-based GWAS of common variants to a family-based design targeting rare variants in PBC, a strategy that remains largely unexplored in this disease despite its known heritability. It leverages Dr. Abecasis's expertise in genetic association methods and rare variant analysis in a novel phenotypic context.

proposals.title: Identification of Putative Protective Variants Against Primary Biliary Cholangitis via Large-Scale Exome-Wide Association and Burden Testing
proposals.motivation: Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC) is a chronic autoimmune liver disease with a strong, well-characterized genetic component. While genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully identified numerous common variants associated with increased disease risk, the systematic search for genetic variants that confer strong protection against PBC remains underexplored. Identifying such protective variants, especially rare, coding variants with large effect sizes, could reveal novel biological pathways for therapeutic intervention and provide critical insights into disease resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare, coding loss-of-function (LoF) or missense variants in specific genes, which are significantly depleted in PBC cases compared to controls, confer strong protection against disease development.
proposals.method: We will perform an exome-wide association study (ExWAS) and rare variant burden analysis in a large, deeply phenotyped cohort of PBC cases and matched controls. Leveraging exome or whole-genome sequencing data, we will apply the SAIGE-GENE framework for scalable mixed-model association tests of rare variants, aggregating variants by gene. We will specifically test for protective associations by identifying genes where rare, predicted deleterious variants (e.g., pLoF, missense with CADD > 20) are significantly less frequent in cases than controls. Candidate genes will be prioritized using pre-2026 functional annotations and protein-protein interaction networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic catalog of rare genetic variants associated with protection from PBC, moving beyond risk-focused genetics. It will nominate high-confidence target genes and biological pathways for functional validation and drug development aimed at mimicking protective biology. The findings will also enhance genetic risk prediction by identifying resilient individuals.
proposals.novelty: This proposal directly inverts the standard paradigm of genetic association studies for autoimmune disease from seeking risk factors to seeking protective factors, applying state-of-the-art rare variant association methods specifically to detect depletion signals, which is a distinct analytical challenge from detecting enrichment.

ideas.title: Prioritize and Validate Protective-Variant-Inspired Druggable Targets in Clinical Trials
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the search for protective genetic variants with the clinical testing of druggable targets. It begins by systematically identifying rare or low-frequency variants associated with a markedly reduced disease risk, specifically focusing on genes within known causal pathways like SELE, ICAM5, CD209, and coagulation factor F8. The core innovation is to use these human-derived protective variants as a prioritization mechanism, selecting for clinical trials only those druggable targets whose modulation is predicted to mimic a naturally occurring, protective genetic state. This approach aims to de-risk clinical development by grounding therapeutic hypotheses in human genetics evidence for resilience, rather than just disease association.

ideas.title: Systematic identification of protective homozygous LoF variants through case-control depletion analysis

This directly tests the hypothesis from Idea B—that some homozygous LoF variants can be beneficial—using the protective variant detection framework from Idea A. The approach moves beyond cataloging tolerated LoF variants to pinpointing those with a demonstrable negative association with disease, providing strong statistical evidence for a protective effect. Identified variants can then be prioritized for functional validation to uncover novel mechanisms of genetic resilience and potential therapeutic targets.

ideas.title: Ancestry-Conditioned Protective Variant Discovery (AC-PVD)
ideas.description: This system identifies putatively protective genetic variants by directly modeling and correcting for population stratification, the primary confounder of case-control frequency comparisons. It employs a two-stage framework: first, a high-resolution ancestry assignment clusters individuals using genome-wide data, creating matched genetic backgrounds. Second, association tests for protection are performed within these homogeneous clusters, comparing variant frequencies between cases and controls while conditioning on fine-scale ancestry coordinates. This method isolates true protective signals from spurious ones caused by uneven sampling from diverse populations, significantly increasing the robustness and replicability of discovered variants.

ideas.title: Excess Homozygous LoF Burden is Deleterious Within Families After Controlling for Genetic Background and Validating Functional Impact
ideas.description: This study directly tests whether an individual's excess of homozygous loss-of-function (LoF) variants, relative to their own family's genetic background, leads to measurable phenotypic detriment. By sequencing parent-offspring quads, it compares siblings discordant for homozygosity burden, controlling for population stratification and shared environment. Putatively deleterious homozygous LoF variants are then validated using induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived models from these siblings to confirm their functional impact on gene expression and cellular pathways. This design robustly separates the effects of genetic drift from selection, providing direct evidence for the deleteriousness of excess homozygosity in a controlled genetic context.

ideas.title: Modeling Time-Varying Genetic Penetrance in COVID-19 Using Survival Analysis and Time-Series GWAS
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of constant genetic effect sizes by proposing that the penetrance of COVID-19-associated genetic variants may be time-dependent, varying across stages of disease progression. Drawing an analogy from oncology, where the influence of risk alleles like BRCA1/2 on cancer onset is modeled as a function of age using survival analysis (e.g., Cox models with time-dependent covariates), the adapted solution applies these time-to-event and time-series GWAS methods to COVID-19. The research would treat clinical milestones (e.g., time to hospitalization, time to ICU admission) as events, analyzing longitudinal patient genomic data to test whether the hazard ratios for specific genetic variants significantly change over the course of the infection, thereby revealing dynamic genetic architecture.

ideas.title: Reference-free co-estimation of ancestral haplotypes and phasing in admixed genomes using long-read sequencing
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the critical flaw of reference panel dependency by developing a computational system that performs joint inference of haplotype phase and ancestral haplotype structure directly from long-read sequencing data of an admixed population. The system employs a hierarchical clustering and hidden Markov model (HMM) to identify and assemble shared, long-range haplotype blocks across individuals, effectively building an internal reference panel representative of the ancestral diversity present in the sample. This co-estimation framework enables highly accurate phasing even for ancestries with poorly documented genetic variation, as it does not require prior LD information. The resulting phased haplotypes and inferred ancestral segments provide a robust foundation for downstream admixture mapping and trait association studies in underrepresented populations.

ideas.title: Using Gradient Exposure Controls to Identify Pathogenic Variants
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from environmental toxicology, where gradient exposure controls are used to isolate specific pathogenic mechanisms. The analogous problem is determining whether a health outcome is due to a specific high-level exposure or a general background risk. By applying this to COVID-19 genetics, we propose using a gradient of control groups based on infection severity and immune status (asymptomatic, mild, seropositive without symptoms, and naive) rather than a single population control. This refined comparison will help identify genetic variants or immune markers specifically associated with the dysregulated response leading to severe disease, filtering out variants merely associated with infection susceptibility or common population background noise.

ideas.title: Astronomical Survey-Inspired Deep Phenotyping: Using a 'Deep Field' Cohort to Map Ancestry-Specific Molecular Universes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the astronomical 'Deep Field' strategy to human genomics. Astronomers gain deep cosmic insight by intensively studying a tiny patch of sky with multiple observational techniques, rather than surveying the whole sky shallowly. Analogously, this research will perform exhaustive multi-omics profiling (genomics, epigenomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, microbiomics) on a deeply phenotyped, small subset of individuals from specific genetic ancestry groups. This 'Deep Field' cohort will be characterized with longitudinal clinical data, advanced imaging, and detailed environmental exposure histories. The goal is to create a foundational, high-resolution reference model that definitively connects genetic ancestry to molecular trait networks and regulatory mechanisms. The insights and analytical pipelines developed from this intensive, scaled-down study will then serve as a blueprint for interpreting data from larger, more scalable but shallower population-wide biobanks.

ideas.title: Applying Climate Attribution Models to Genetic Risk Trajectories
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'detection and attribution' framework from climate science to longitudinal genetic and health data. In climate science, long-term observational data is analyzed with complex models to statistically attribute trends (like warming) to specific causes (like greenhouse gases), separating them from natural variability. Here, the longitudinal health outcomes from the prospective cohort are treated as the observed record. By leveraging the new genetic resource to define high-risk genetic profiles, researchers can model counterfactual health trajectories—simulating what would have happened with a lower genetic risk burden, akin to a 'natural forcing only' climate model. The comparison between observed and simulated trajectories allows for the formal attribution of disease risk, onset timing, and progression patterns to specific genetic architectures, quantifying their effect size over time while accounting for other longitudinal variables like environment and behavior.

ideas.title: Disentangling Time-Dependent Genetic Effects from Treatment and Pathophysiological Confounding in COVID-19 Progression
ideas.description: This study establishes a deeply phenotyped, longitudinal cohort of COVID-19 patients with serial biospecimen collection and clinical data capture at predefined disease stages. It employs a causal inference framework to rigorously test if genetic variant associations with outcomes (e.g., severity scores, biomarker levels) change over time, independent of time-varying treatments and pathophysiological states. The design explicitly models measured confounders like antiviral drug administration, corticosteroid use, and secondary infections as time-dependent covariates in mixed-effects models. Sensitivity analyses and negative control outcomes are used to quantify and bound the potential impact of residual confounding, ensuring that any declared 'time-dependent genetic effect' is robust to these major non-genetic influences.

ideas.title: Bias-aware active learning for iterative deep learning prioritization of non-coding variants
ideas.description: This system employs a deep learning model trained on genomic and epigenomic data to score non-coding variants, but crucially couples it with an active learning module that quantifies prediction uncertainty and identifies regions of genomic context space where the model is poorly calibrated. The most uncertain variants from these underrepresented contexts are prioritized for targeted functional assays (e.g., MPRA). The resulting experimental data is then used to retrain the model in the next cycle. This closed-loop approach systematically reduces dataset bias, improves generalizability, and ensures the prioritization algorithm becomes more robust and comprehensive over time.

ideas.title: Precision immunomodulation for critical COVID-19 subtypes by targeting distinct causal pathways
ideas.description: This research program first defines discrete molecular subtypes of critical COVID-19 (e.g., hyperinflammatory, endothelial, thrombotic, or combinations thereof) using deep clinical and multi-omic phenotyping. It then designs and executes adaptive platform trials where patients are stratified into these subtypes and assigned to targeted interventions—such as SELE/ICAM5 inhibitors for a vascular adhesion subtype or refined anticoagulant strategies for a dominant coagulation (F8-related) subtype—rather than testing each drug in an unselected population. This approach simultaneously validates the clinical relevance of the subtypes and directly tests whether precision immunomodulation based on causal pathway dominance improves outcomes in critical illness.

ideas.title: Excess homozygous LoF variants as context-dependent 'genetic shadows' requiring ecological assessment
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that excess homozygous loss-of-function (LoF) variants are uniformly deleterious by drawing an analogy from community ecology. In ecology, the impact of a species (analogous to a genetic variant) is not intrinsic but depends entirely on its context—the ecosystem (analogous to the genetic and environmental background). The analogous solution involves ditching binary labels for a functional, context-dependent assessment framework. Adapted to genetics, this means developing models and experiments that do not ask 'Is this homozygous LoF bad?' but 'Under what specific genetic, epigenetic, microbial, and environmental conditions is this homozygous LoF state neutral, harmful, or beneficial?' This shifts the research focus to defining the multidimensional niches that shape the fitness landscape for these variants.

ideas.title: Causal phenotyping of critical COVID-19: Linking mechanistic subtypes to differential outcomes and therapeutic implications
ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive clustering to establish causal, actionable subtypes of critical COVID-19. We integrate deep longitudinal clinical data with multi-omics profiling from large, prospective cohorts to identify patient subgroups defined by distinct pathophysiological drivers and clinical trajectories. Each candidate subtype is rigorously validated by demonstrating a unique association with a specific clinical outcome (e.g., rapid respiratory decline vs. progressive thrombotic complications) and by mapping its molecular signature to a prioritized set of druggable pathways. The final output is a clinically deployable subtyping tool and a shortlist of subtype-specific therapeutic candidates for targeted clinical trials.

ideas.title: A Computational-Experimental Framework for Deconvolving and Validating Lung and Immune Cell-Specific Expression from Bulk Omics Data
ideas.description: This project creates a robust pipeline to move beyond merely challenging bulk data to actively resolving it. It employs reference single-cell atlases to build cell-type signature matrices and applies statistical deconvolution algorithms to bulk RNA-seq or proteomic data from lung tissue or blood, quantifying the specific expression profiles masked within. The computationally inferred cell-type-specific expression is then rigorously validated using orthogonal methods such as flow-sorted cell RNA sequencing or multiplexed spatial transcriptomics on matched samples. This integrated approach provides a concrete, falsifiable method to replace assumptions about tissue specificity with quantitative, validated estimates.

ideas.title: Functional phenotyping of homozygous LoF variants using a population genetics-informed knockout array
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Conservation Ecology's Population Viability Analysis (PVA) to functional genomics. In PVA, models simulate the impact of accumulated genetic load on the future survival of small populations. The adapted solution applies this 'population at risk' logic to cellular populations. We propose to experimentally test the phenotypic impact of homozygous LoF variants in Indigenous Mexican genomic segments by creating a CRISPR knockout array that systematically targets these haplotypes in a controlled cellular model. Each edited cell line represents a 'population' with a specific genetic load. High-throughput cellular phenotyping—measuring growth, viability, and response to metabolic and environmental stressors—serves as the analog to population trajectory modeling. This approach functionally validates whether these variant-rich segments, potentially under selection in specific populations, confer a net neutral, deleterious, or context-dependent fitness effect at the cellular level, revealing their biological resilience or vulnerability.

ideas.title: Robust demographic inference via integrated IBD and local ancestry concordance modeling
ideas.description: This approach creates a robust system for inferring recent population history by jointly modeling Identity-by-Descent (IBD) segments and local ancestry tract lengths. It uses a simulation-based framework that iteratively proposes demographic models, simulates expected genetic outcomes, and compares them to observed data from both IBD detectors and local ancestry inference tools. The method identifies the demographic model that maximizes the joint likelihood of the observed IBD and ancestry patterns, effectively using each signal to correct for errors and biases in the other. This results in more accurate and reliable inference of recent admixture times, effective population sizes, and migration rates, even from noisy or sparsely sampled data.

ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive mechanism listing to build a quantitative, dynamic model that predicts disease progression. It integrates serial blood and tissue multi-omics data (transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics) from well-phenotyped COVID-19 cohorts into a computational framework for causal network inference. The model will identify driver mechanisms, their sequence of activation, and their relative weights in determining severe outcomes. Finally, it will be validated by its ability to predict individual patient trajectories and in silico responses to mechanism-targeted interventions.

ideas.title: Adapting Legal Precedent Search NLP for Biomedical Variant Annotation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Legal Informatics, where NLP is used to search case law and legal documents for relevant precedents to inform new legal arguments. The analogous solution involves parsing unstructured legal text to identify key entities, relationships, and the authoritative weight of prior decisions through citation analysis. Adapted to genomic research, the proposed system would employ similar NLP techniques to mine the biomedical literature for prior evidence on newly discovered genetic variants in a specific population. It would identify and link gene/variant mentions, extract evidence type and direction from text, and apply a network-based 'precedent' scoring model that considers publication authority and citation frequency to automatically generate a ranked, evidence-backed annotation for each variant, significantly accelerating the interpretation process in population genomics studies.

ideas.title: Establish a Global Genetic Reference Network through Decentralized Consortium Models
ideas.description: The original idea to expand genomic studies to diverse ancestries faces challenges of insufficient sample sizes, fragmented resources, and lack of standardized methods in non-European populations. Drawing an analogy from High-Energy Physics, where discoveries require massive, collaborative efforts like those at CERN, we propose adapting the decentralized consortium model. The analogous solution involves independent entities (nations, institutes) pooling resources, standardizing protocols, and sharing infrastructure around a common goal. Adapted to genomics, this means establishing a Global Genetic Reference Network—a federated consortium of regional biobanks and research centers across continents. Members would adopt shared, interoperable standards for phenotyping, genotyping, and data analysis to ensure comparability. This structure aggregates locally managed cohorts into a globally powerful resource, enabling the replication and discovery of genetic associations with the statistical rigor required for global relevance, while maintaining ethical and data sovereignty frameworks appropriate to each population.

ideas.title: Spatio-Network Mapping of Ancestry-Associated Disease Pathways
ideas.description: This research direction integrates geostatistical kriging of fine-scale ancestry patterns with network propagation on protein interaction networks. It first creates high-resolution maps of genetic ancestry across a region like Mexico, then uses these maps to stratify and weight genetic association signals based on local ancestry structure. These spatially contextualized genetic signals are then propagated through biological networks to discover ancestry-informed disease pathways, revealing mechanisms that may be geographically localized or differentially active in specific ancestral backgrounds. The approach moves beyond mapping ancestry or pathways in isolation to modeling how the spatial distribution of ancestry shapes the network architecture of disease risk.

ideas.title: Functional Constraint Mapping via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that lower coding variation in Indigenous segments inherently indicates reduced genetic diversity or evolutionary potential. By drawing an analogy from Ecology and Biogeography, we adapt the solution of Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM). In ecology, low genetic diversity in a population is often explained by specialization to a local environmental niche, not just demographic history. The adapted research idea proposes to map the landscape of historical functional constraints (e.g., specific pathogens, dietary resources, thermal stress) acting on an Indigenous population. The lower observed coding variation is then framed not as a simple deficit, but as a genomic signature of adaptation to that unique constraint landscape. The solution involves modeling the population's genomic profile as an optimal fit to its functional 'niche,' predicting that the retained variation, though less diverse, is highly enriched for alleles critical to that niche. This reinterprets the variation pattern from a deficit to a specialized adaptation.

ideas.title: Apply error-correcting code reconstruction from digital communications to haplotype phasing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from digital communications—using structured redundancy and iterative probabilistic decoding to reconstruct a signal from noisy data—to the problem of haplotype phasing in admixed genomes. In digital communications, error-correcting codes and algorithms like belief propagation are used to recover the original transmitted message over a noisy channel. Analogously, an admixed genome can be viewed as a 'message' where the haplotypes (parental chromosomal segments) have a specific population-genetic structure, and sequencing data provides a noisy, fragmented observation. The adapted research idea is to develop a haplotype phasing algorithm that explicitly models the expected haplotype block patterns (from reference panels or de novo) as a 'codebook' or constraint graph. It would then employ an iterative, message-passing decoding algorithm, inspired by those used for Low-Density Parity-Check (LDPC) codes, to optimally integrate long-read/linked-read data and population genetic priors. This approach would be particularly powerful for resolving phase in regions with low coverage or high heterozygosity by leveraging global genomic context, much like a decoder uses the entire codeword structure to correct local errors.

A secure federated analysis platform allows researchers to run approved algorithms across nodes without transferring individual-level data. This structure ensures scientific comparability, respects diverse legal and consent frameworks, and provides a robust, scalable model for continental-scale research.

ideas.title: Adaptive Platform Trial Design for Multi-Target Neurovascular Therapeutics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the efficient, multi-target platform trial design pioneered in oncology (e.g., I-SPY 2, Lung-MAP) to the challenge of testing multiple druggable targets (SELE, ICAM5, CD209, F8) in neurovascular disease. The analogous oncology solution uses a single master protocol with a shared infrastructure and control group to test several targeted therapies in parallel within biomarker-defined patient subgroups, using adaptive rules to focus resources on the most promising candidates. The adapted research idea is to develop and implement a similar adaptive platform trial for neurovascular therapeutics. This would involve creating a master protocol that simultaneously evaluates therapeutics against these distinct causal pathways in a patient population with a common neurovascular condition. Patients would be stratified by biomarker profiles (e.g., endothelial activation vs. coagulation dysregulation) and assigned to corresponding targeted therapy arms. The adaptive design allows for interim analyses to drop futile interventions and potentially expand enrollment for the most effective targets, dramatically accelerating the evaluation of multiple causal pathway hypotheses within a single, efficient clinical trial framework.

ideas.title: Ancestry-agnostic modeling of admixture as a continuum of shared haplotypes under spatiotemporal selection
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond the paradigm of modeling admixture as a mixture of discrete ancestral populations. It uses a graph representation of haplotype sharing across modern and ancient genomes to infer a continuum of ancestral genetic structure. A deep generative model, trained on spatially explicit simulations of population dynamics over realistic landscapes, reconstructs admixture histories and selection pressures without assigning individuals to fixed ancestral groups. The system is validated by testing its ability to predict the genetic variation and adaptive loci found in independently dated ancient DNA samples, treating them as dynamic snapshots rather than static ancestral proxies.

ideas.title: Context-constrained network propagation for cell-type-specific pathway discovery
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a single aggregated network by constructing a multi-layer network framework that incorporates cell-type and tissue-specific protein interaction data derived from transcriptomic and proteomic resources. Genetic hits are propagated under constraints defined by these contextual layers, ensuring signal diffusion is primarily confined to interactions active in the disease-relevant biological context. This prevents the identification of spuriously strong connections that are artifacts of network aggregation rather than true biology. The output is a set of prioritized pathway connections that are explicitly linked to their operative cellular environment, dramatically improving biological interpretability and validation potential.

ideas.title: Adjudicated Evidence Integration for Variant Annotation via NLP and Multi-Source Confidence Scoring
ideas.description: This system uses NLP to mine biomedical literature for variant mentions but directly addresses the critical flaw of extracting unverified claims. It integrates the extracted text into an adjudication pipeline that automatically cross-references each assertion against high-confidence sources like ClinVar, practice guidelines, and large-scale functional screens. A probabilistic confidence model, trained on expert-curated benchmarks, scores each piece of evidence based on source authority, publication date, and corroboration across sources. The final output is a dynamically updated variant report that clearly separates high-confidence annotations from low-confidence or conflicting mentions, preventing the automated propagation of erroneous evidence.

ideas.title: Adapting Geochemical Interpolation Methods for Ancestry Mapping
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from geochemistry and environmental science, where multivariate geostatistical methods like cokriging are used to interpolate the concentrations of multiple, correlated chemical elements from sparse soil samples to create continuous spatial maps. The analogous problem is predicting the spatial distribution of multiple, compositionally-constrained variables (where proportions sum to a constant) across a landscape. The adapted idea applies this multivariate kriging framework to genetic ancestry data. Using cohort data from Mexico with known geographic origins, the method would model not only the spatial autocorrelation of individual ancestry components (e.g., Indigenous, European) but also the cross-correlations between them. This allows for the simultaneous interpolation of all ancestry proportions across the country, generating a set of fine-scale maps that are spatially smooth and internally consistent, ensuring predicted ancestry proportions at any location are valid (non-negative and sum to 1), thereby providing a more robust and nuanced picture of ancestry geography than univariate interpolation of each component separately.

ideas.title: Enhance imputation panel via federated learning from distributed biobanks
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where the Event Horizon Telescope synthesizes data from distributed observatories to achieve unprecedented resolution, this idea proposes a federated learning solution for genomic imputation. The analogous problem of needing a massive, singular instrument was solved by creating a synchronized network of independent telescopes. Adapted to genomics, the solution is to architect a secure, federated system where numerous biobanks and genomic consortia collaboratively train a next-generation imputation reference panel. Each participant contributes insights from their local whole-genome sequences—preserving data privacy—to iteratively build a unified, globally representative model. This federated approach dramatically expands the effective sample size and diversity of the panel, enabling highly accurate imputation of even very rare and population-specific variants without the logistical and ethical hurdles of centralizing all the raw genomic data.

ideas.title: Inverse Admixture Tomography: Reconstructing Historical Population Dynamics from Genomic Scattering Signatures
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem-solving framework from cosmology, specifically the analysis of the Cosmic Microwave Background, to population genetics. In cosmology, the detailed pattern of anisotropies in the CMB—a scattering signature—is used to infer the fundamental parameters and sequence of events in the early universe. Analogously, we propose to treat the complex, anisotropic patterns of genetic admixture observed in modern Mexican populations as a genomic 'scattering surface' resulting from historical demographic processes. The solution is to build a detailed generative model that simulates population splits, size changes, and migration waves over time, which produces simulated admixture patterns. Using advanced statistical inference and model comparison techniques (e.g., Approximate Bayesian Computation or deep learning-based emulators), we will invert the observed data to find the most likely historical parameters—such as the timing, magnitude, and sources of migration pulses and population bottlenecks—that generated the present-day genomic landscape. This moves the field from describing admixture patterns to quantitatively reconstructing the detailed historical narrative that created them.

ideas.title: Apply Document Version Control Methods to IBD Segment Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from software engineering and document management to the problem of inferring recent population history using Identity-by-Descent (IBD) segments. In version control systems, the history of a codebase is reconstructed by comparing different file versions and identifying shared blocks of text that indicate common ancestry, which are then used to build a directed acyclic graph of commits, branches, and merges. We propose to treat IBD segments in a population similarly: each shared segment is analogous to a 'commit' from an ancestral haplotype. By developing algorithms that analyze the complex patterns of sharing, length, divergence, and overlap of IBD segments across a large sample, we can construct a probabilistic, temporal graph of population events. This graph would explicitly model the sequence of recent population splits, admixture (merges), and effective population size changes, moving beyond static relatedness matrices to infer a dynamic history of lineage divergence and convergence.

ideas.title: Adapt ecological network stability analysis to map COVID-19 pathophysiology interactions
ideas.description: This research adapts ecological network stability analysis to investigate the multi-component pathophysiology model of critical COVID-19. Drawing from ecology—where complex ecosystems are modeled as interaction networks to identify keystone species and tipping points—we propose treating distinct COVID-19 biological mechanisms (viral dynamics, immune hyperinflammation, endothelial injury, coagulopathy) as network nodes and their pathophysiological interactions as edges. By applying ecological stability analysis techniques, we can systematically map which specific interactions or combinations of mechanisms are most destabilizing, pushing the patient's physiological system toward the critical 'tipping point' of organ failure and death. This approach provides a formal framework to quantify interaction strengths, identify therapeutic targets within the interaction network, and predict which patients are approaching pathophysiological instability.

ideas.title: Admixture as a Reaction-Diffusion System: Modeling Selection as a Continuous Chemical Potential
ideas.description: This idea challenges the static, discrete-block assumption in admixture modeling by drawing an analogy from physical chemistry. In that field, complex mixtures like alloys or polymer blends are not simple sums; their formation and final structure are governed by dynamic reaction-diffusion processes and chemical potentials that dictate component interactions. Adapting this, we propose modeling genetic admixture as a dynamic system where ancestral genomic components 'diffuse' across generations and 'react' under a continuous landscape of selective pressure. This selective potential function, replacing the concept of discrete, locally acting selection on fixed blocks, would drive the non-equilibrium dynamics of ancestry, predicting how admixture proportions reorganize over time in response to selection, migration, and drift in a way that a simple linear sum cannot capture.
